Unlike the classical Gaussian uplink multiple access channel, where RS recovers the time-sharing dominant face, the split factor in uplink ISAC also reshapes the sensing-stage interference, allowing the RS-inspired boundary to match or strictly enlarge the S&C tradeoff. High-SNR analysis shows that, for non-aligned S&C channels, residual sensing interference changes the rate offsets but not the leading S&C slopes, whereas in the fully-aligned case it becomes slope-limiting. Using an aperture-aware near-field channel model, large-array limits are derived, showing that achievable rates remain finite as the array grows. Numerical results validate the analysis and demonstrate the benefits of the RS-inspired scheme, the impact of residual sensing interference, and the bounded large-array behaviour induced by physically consistent near-field modelling.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a rate-splitting (RS)-inspired framework for uplink near-field ISAC, deriving closed-form rate expressions and showing that the RS-inspired approach can match or outperform NOMA-inspired schemes, with analysis of residual sensing interference and large-array limits.
AI.motivation: To address the fundamental receiver-design challenge in uplink ISAC caused by inter-functionality interference between sensing and communication, and to overcome limitations of existing NOMA-inspired frameworks.
AI.method: Develops an RS-inspired framework that generalizes sensing-centric and communication-centric NOMA endpoints by splitting communication messages across sensing operations; derives closed-form CR and SR expressions accounting for residual sensing interference; analyzes high-SNR and large-array asymptotic behavior.
AI.result: The RS-inspired boundary contains the NOMA-inspired time-sharing region; split factor reshapes sensing-stage interference, enabling matching or strict enlargement of the S&C tradeoff; residual sensing interference changes rate offsets (non-aligned channels) or becomes slope-limiting (fully-aligned); achievable rates remain finite as array grows under physically consistent near-field modeling.
AI.conclusion: The RS-inspired framework provides superior S&C performance tradeoffs compared to NOMA-inspired approaches in uplink near-field ISAC, with bounded large-array behavior due to physically consistent modeling.

title: DaX: Learning General Pathology Representations Across Scales
summary: Computational pathology requires visual representations that transfer across diverse clinical endpoints and remain robust to variation in magnification, staining, scanner type, slide preparation, and input resolution. We present DaX, a pathology vision foundation model that adapts DINOv3-style self-supervised learning to whole-slide histopathology. DaX is initialized from natural-image DINOv3 weights and incorporates continuous magnification training, cross-scale tissue views, orientation-agnostic and acquisition-robust augmentation, multi-input-size training, and Gram-anchored dense consistency. These designs aim to connect local cellular morphology with global tissue architecture while stabilizing dense token-level representations across input scales. We further construct a WSI-level benchmark comprising 161 clinically meaningful tasks from 44 public datasets, covering 28,182 patients and 34,394 slides across four clinical domains and nine task categories. All models are evaluated under a fixed patient-level cross-validation protocol with fold-level statistical ranking, enabling reproducible comparisons that are less sensitive to split-dependent variation. Across this benchmark, DaX achieves the highest mean performance across tasks and consistently strong task-level ranking scores, with gains spanning diagnostic pathology, biomarker and molecular profiling, tissue/specimen context, and risk, response, and prognosis. These results support DaX as a transferable visual encoder for computational pathology and provide a standardized evaluation framework for future pathology foundation models. Project page: https://alibaba-damo-academy.github.io/DaX/benchboard/.

AI.tldr: DaX is a pathology vision foundation model adapting DINOv3-style self-supervised learning to whole-slide histopathology, achieving top performance across a benchmark of 161 clinical tasks spanning diagnostic pathology, biomarker profiling, tissue context, and prognosis.
AI.motivation: Computational pathology requires visual representations that transfer across diverse clinical endpoints and remain robust to variations in magnification, staining, scanner type, slide preparation, and input resolution. Existing models lack such robustness and transferability.
AI.method: DaX adapts DINOv3-style self-supervised learning to histopathology by initializing from natural-image DINOv3 weights and incorporating continuous magnification training, cross-scale tissue views, orientation-agnostic/augmentation-robust training, multi-input-size training, and Gram-anchored dense consistency to connect local cellular morphology with global tissue architecture.
AI.result: Across a benchmark of 161 clinically meaningful tasks from 44 public datasets (28,182 patients, 34,394 slides), DaX achieves the highest mean performance and strongest task-level ranking scores across diagnostic pathology, biomarker/molecular profiling, tissue/specimen context, and risk/response/prognosis domains.
AI.conclusion: DaX serves as a transferable visual encoder for computational pathology, and the study provides a standardized evaluation framework for future pathology foundation models.

title: Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT): Fully Realising a GPU-based Symmetric Pseudo-Boolean SAT Solver
summary: We present Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT), a GPU-accelerated solver for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability based on continuous local search (CLS). AFSAT realises the proof-of-concept approach, FastFourierSAT, into a fully-engineered solver supporting any heterogeneous mixture of symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance. Using the JAX compiler, AFSAT leverages pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and just-in-time (JIT) compilation to perform massively parallel CLS across batches of candidate assignments. We demonstrate substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the proof-of-concept. We achieve this by way of identifying and addressing various limitations that arise from memory latency and floating-point representation, as well as leveraging automatic parallelisation and compact representations. The inherent representational and stability limitations of floating point are partially addressed by a tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation. We achieve near-linear throughput when scaling to multiple accelerators via JAX array sharding.

AI.tldr: AFSAT is a GPU-accelerated pseudo-Boolean SAT solver using continuous local search, built on JAX. It improves upon FastFourierSAT with better stability, performance, memory efficiency, and near-linear multi-GPU scaling.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of the proof-of-concept FastFourierSAT approach—specifically numerical instability, memory latency, floating-point representation issues—and to create a fully-engineered solver that handles heterogeneous constraint types efficiently via GPU acceleration.
AI.method: Built with JAX compiler, leveraging pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and JIT compilation for massively parallel CLS across batches. Uses a tailored DFT implementation to mitigate floating-point limitations, and JAX array sharding for multi-accelerator scaling.
AI.result: Substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the proof-of-concept. Achieves near-linear throughput when scaling to multiple accelerators.
AI.conclusion: AFSAT successfully transforms a proof-of-concept into a practical, high-performance GPU-accelerated solver, overcoming key floating-point and scalability challenges.

title: The Synthesis-Sequencing Channel for DNA-based Data Storage
summary: We introduce and study the synthesis-sequencing channel, a two-stage model for DNA-based data storage that jointly captures synthesis and sequencing effects. The synthesis-sequencing channel provides a more nuanced and realistic model of the DNA storage process compared to prior work, as it distinguishes between physical coverage after synthesis and sequencing coverage after readout, relaxes the assumption of independent errors across reads, and naturally induces coverage bias through the composition of synthesis and sequencing stages. We establish the information-theoretic capacity of this channel by deriving matching converse and achievability bounds for the case where synthesis and sequencing errors are modeled by binary symmetric channels with possibly different error probabilities, under mild assumptions on the channel parameters. Our results reveal multiple trade-offs between physical coverage, synthesis errors, sequencing coverage, and sequencing errors that influence the maximum achievable rate for reliable data storage.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a synthesis-sequencing channel model for DNA-based data storage that captures both synthesis and sequencing effects, and establishes its information-theoretic capacity with matching bounds under binary symmetric channel error models.
AI.motivation: Prior DNA storage models oversimplify the process by assuming independent errors across reads and ignoring the distinction between physical coverage (after synthesis) and sequencing coverage (after readout). A more realistic model is needed to capture coverage bias and the two-stage nature of DNA storage.
AI.method: The authors model DNA storage as a two-stage channel (synthesis followed by sequencing), where both stages are binary symmetric channels with possibly different error probabilities. They derive matching converse and achievability bounds under mild assumptions on channel parameters.
AI.result: The information-theoretic capacity of the synthesis-sequencing channel is established. The results reveal multiple trade-offs between physical coverage, synthesis errors, sequencing coverage, and sequencing errors that affect the maximum achievable rate for reliable storage.
AI.conclusion: The synthesis-sequencing channel provides a more nuanced model for DNA storage, and its capacity analysis yields insights into the fundamental limits and trade-offs governing reliable DNA-based data storage.

title: AxisGuide: Grounding Robot Action Coordinate System in RGB Observations for Robust Visuomotor Manipulation
comment: Accepted to Robotics: Science and Systems (RSS) 2026
summary: Visuomotor manipulation policies trained via large-scale behavior cloning have achieved strong semantic scene understanding, yet often fail to reliably execute correct low-level actions under distribution shifts. For example, even in a simple pickup task with identical scene layouts, camera viewpoints, and illumination, performance can degrade substantially when the object is placed at unseen locations. We argue that this gap arises from insufficient action understanding, namely the inability to interpret the robot's base-frame action coordinate system in image space. To address this issue, we introduce AxisGuide, a lightweight guidance method that bridges semantic scene understanding and action-coordinate interpretation. Using camera parameters and end-effector poses, AxisGuide renders the robot base-frame axes in each camera view and augments RGB observations with a small set of cue channels that explicitly visualize the meaning of the +x, +y, and +z motions in image space. Extensive evaluations in both the LIBERO simulation and real-world environments demonstrate that AxisGuide yields substantial performance gains and improved generalization, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit action-coordinate cues for learning reliable and transferable generalist visuomotor policies.

AI.tldr: AxisGuide is a lightweight method that augments RGB observations with rendered robot base-frame axes to improve visuomotor policies' action-coordinate understanding, yielding significant performance gains in both simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Visuomotor manipulation policies fail under distribution shifts (e.g., object at unseen locations) due to insufficient action understanding — specifically the inability to interpret the robot's base-frame action coordinate system in image space.
AI.method: Using camera parameters and end-effector poses, AxisGuide renders the robot base-frame axes (x, y, z) in each camera view and augments RGB observations with additional cue channels that explicitly visualize the meaning of motions in image space.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations in LIBERO simulation and real-world environments show substantial performance gains and improved generalization compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit action-coordinate cues are effective for learning reliable and transferable generalist visuomotor policies.

title: Architecture-Adaptive Uncertainty Fusion for Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfake detection systems achieve near-perfect accuracy on benchmarks, yet forensic deployment demands reliable prediction uncertainty. Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods rely on single sources and ignore that optimal uncertainty composition varies across architectures. We propose Correlation-Optimized Fusion (COF), an architecture-adaptive framework that fuses five complementary uncertainty sources -- epistemic, aleatoric, calibration, conformal, and distributional -- by maximizing Pearson correlation between fused uncertainty scores and prediction errors via constrained optimization on the probability simplex. COF requires no model modifications and only 42 s of weight optimization, compared to 20--45 h for a 5-model Deep Ensemble. Evaluation across eleven architectures on FaceForensics++ reveals a fundamental trade-off: under matched train/evaluation protocol, non-linear methods achieve approximately 5--6% higher in-domain correlation than COF (mean r = 0.438), but this reverses under distribution shift. On CelebDF, COF outperforms Random Forest in 9/11 architectures with up to 7.3x higher correlation (MaxViT-B: r = 0.249 vs. 0.034); RF degrades 85% cross-domain to r = 0.071, whereas COF retains substantially more signal (74% drop to r = 0.116). Cross-dataset evaluation on CelebDF and DFDC reveals catastrophic generalization failure across all methods: in-domain correlations of 0.41--0.47 collapse to near-zero externally (mean degradation 90.7%), with seven of eleven architectures exhibiting uncertainty inversion. These results establish COF as a practical, interpretable framework for controlled-distribution deployment and identify domain-adaptive UQ as the central open challenge for forensic deployment.

AI.tldr: COF is an architecture-adaptive framework that fuses five complementary uncertainty sources for deepfake detection, achieving practical performance without model modifications, but revealing catastrophic generalization failure under cross-domain shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing UQ methods rely on single uncertainty sources and ignore that optimal uncertainty composition varies across architectures, limiting reliable deployment in forensic settings.
AI.method: Proposes Correlation-Optimized Fusion (COF), which fuses five uncertainty sources (epistemic, aleatoric, calibration, conformal, distributional) by maximizing Pearson correlation between fused scores and prediction errors via constrained optimization on the probability simplex.
AI.result: COF requires only 42s of optimization vs. 20-45h for Deep Ensembles. Under distribution shift, COF outperforms RF in 9/11 architectures (up to 7.3x higher correlation). However, cross-dataset evaluation shows catastrophic failure: correlations of 0.41-0.47 collapse to near-zero (90.7% degradation), with 7/11 architectures showing uncertainty inversion.
AI.conclusion: COF is practical for controlled-distribution deployment, but domain-adaptive UQ remains the central open challenge for real-world forensic deepfake detection.

title: Gaussian Process Latent Factor Regression for Low-Data, High-Dimensional Output Problems
comment: 9 pages content + 22 pages appendix/references. Supporting code at https://github.com/edstevenson/GPLFR
summary: In the sciences, regression tasks often require predicting high-dimensional outputs from few training examples. Multi-output Gaussian processes excel in low-data regimes but typically struggle with high-dimensional outputs. Compress-then-predict pipelines such as PCA-GP (principal component analysis plus Gaussian process regression) handle high dimensionality, but rely on bases optimized for reconstruction rather than prediction. To address this gap, we propose a model that represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state drawn from a Gaussian process prior. By analytically marginalizing the decoder weights, we couple compression and prediction in a single objective that scales to high-dimensional outputs. We refer to this model as Gaussian process latent factor regression (GPLFR). We demonstrate GPLFR by building the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.

AI.tldr: GPLFR couples compression and prediction in multi-output Gaussian processes for high-dimensional regression tasks, demonstrated on exoplanet climate modeling.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-output GPs struggle with high-dimensional outputs, while PCA-GP pipelines optimize bases for reconstruction rather than prediction, creating a gap.
AI.method: Each output is a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state from a GP prior; decoder weights are analytically marginalized to jointly optimize compression and prediction.
AI.result: Built the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets, demonstrating scalability to high-dimensional outputs.
AI.conclusion: GPLFR effectively couples compression and prediction in a single objective, outperforming reconstruction-optimized baselines for high-dimensional regression with limited data.

title: Robust Secure Beamforming for Movable Antenna Enhanced Integrated Sensing and Communications
summary: In this letter, we investigate robust beamforming design for a movable antenna (MA)-enhanced secure integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) system with imperfect eaves?dropping channel state information (CSI). To improve radar sensing performance, we formulate a radar signal-to-interference?plus-noise ratio (SINR) maximization problem by jointly opti?mizing the transmit beamforming and antenna placement while ensuring communication data security. However, the resulting op?timization problem is inherently intractable due to the nonlinea mapping from antenna positions to channel coefficients, as well as the eavesdropper (Eve) channel uncertainty. To handle these challenges, we propose a block coordinate descent (BCD)-based algorithm incorporating successive convex approximation (SCA) and fractional programming (FP) techniques. Simulation results show that our proposed algorithm exhibits fast convergence and achieves a significant improvement in the radar SINR while guaranteeing communication security.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a robust beamforming and antenna placement optimization algorithm for MA-enhanced secure ISAC systems under imperfect eavesdropping CSI, using BCD with SCA and FP techniques to maximize radar SINR while ensuring communication security.
AI.motivation: To improve radar sensing performance in MA-enhanced ISAC systems while ensuring communication data security, despite challenges from nonlinear antenna position mapping and imperfect eavesdropper channel state information.
AI.method: Formulates a radar SINR maximization problem jointly optimizing transmit beamforming and antenna placement under security constraints. Solves the intractable non-convex problem using a BCD-based algorithm incorporating SCA and FP techniques.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm exhibits fast convergence and achieves significant improvement in radar SINR while guaranteeing communication security.
AI.conclusion: The BCD-based algorithm with SCA and FP effectively addresses the challenges of MA-enhanced secure ISAC systems, yielding superior radar performance without compromising security.

title: Beyond Universality: The GCC-FER Dataset and Culture-Aware Adaptation for Dynamic Facial Expression Recognition
summary: Dynamic Facial Expression Recognition (DFER) is a key enabling technology in affective computing, human-computer interaction, and intelligent multimedia systems. Despite the significant influence of cultural nuances on FER performance, most existing FER systems assume that emotional expressions are universally consistent across populations. This variation can be attributed to systematic differences in facial muscle activation patterns across cultures. A major challenge in advancing cross-cultural FER lies in the scarcity of culturally diverse benchmark datasets. To address this, a new hybrid multicultural video dataset termed Global Cross-Cultural Facial Expression Recognition (GCC-FER) is introduced. GCC-FER comprises 23,934 video samples spanning four cultural groups (African, Caucasian, East Asian, and South Asian) across seven basic expressions, combining psychologically supervised in-house data collection for underrepresented populations with rigorous ethnicity filtering of existing sources. To the best of our knowledge, GCC-FER is the first large-scale global cross-cultural DFER dataset designed to address these demographic gaps. Leveraging this dataset, behaviorally grounded cultural priors are derived for each cultural group and a global prior for practical deployment. A Culture-Aware FER (CA-FER) system is proposed to mitigate cultural bias by adaptively recalibrating latent facial representations. Extensive experiments on GCC-FER and DFEW demonstrate that the proposed system consistently improves FER performance across multicultural settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GCC-FER, the first large-scale global cross-cultural Dynamic Facial Expression Recognition dataset with 23,934 videos across four cultural groups, and proposes a Culture-Aware FER (CA-FER) system that uses cultural priors to adaptively recalibrate facial representations, mitigating cultural bias in FER.
AI.motivation: Existing DFER systems assume universal emotional expressions across cultures, neglecting cultural nuances in facial muscle activation patterns. There is a severe scarcity of culturally diverse benchmark datasets, particularly for underrepresented populations.
AI.method: The authors constructed GCC-FER (23,934 videos, 4 cultural groups, 7 expressions) via in-house collection for underrepresented groups and rigorous ethnicity filtering of existing sources. They derived cultural priors and a global prior from the dataset, then proposed CA-FER, which adaptively recalibrates latent facial representations to reduce cultural bias.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on GCC-FER and DFEW show that the proposed CA-FER system consistently improves FER performance across multicultural settings compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The GCC-FER dataset and CA-FER system effectively address cultural bias in DFER, highlighting the importance of culturally diverse data and adaptive bias mitigation strategies for fairer and more robust affective computing.

title: A Study of Parallel Continuous Local Search
summary: We study parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) as a solution approach for Boolean satisfiability problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean (PB) constraints. Here, the $n$-variable PB-satisfiability problem is relaxed to a continuous optimisation problem with a differentiable objective function on an $n$-dimensional hypercube. For satisfiable instances, the global minimisers of this optimisation problem correspond to satisfying assignments of the SAT problem at hand. We present several novel findings via empirical experiments: (i) redundant constraints can inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS shows promise as a sub-solver in hybridised settings, quickly completing partial assignments; and (iii) local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality (i.e., degree of satisfaction), due to saddle-dense objectives where additional solver steps yield diminishing returns. Our findings inform practical uses of CLS for SAT on modern accelerator hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) for solving Boolean satisfiability with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, finding that redundant constraints hinder convergence, CLS works well as a sub-solver in hybrid settings, and local search quickly reaches a stable solution quality distribution due to saddle-dense objectives.
AI.motivation: To explore Continuous Local Search (CLS) as an alternative approach for solving Boolean satisfiability problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, leveraging continuous optimisation on a differentiable objective function to potentially exploit modern accelerator hardware.
AI.method: The authors relax the n-variable PB-satisfiability problem to a continuous optimisation problem with a differentiable objective function on an n-dimensional hypercube, then apply parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS), conducting empirical experiments to study convergence behavior.
AI.result: (i) Redundant constraints inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS shows promise as a sub-solver in hybridised settings, quickly completing partial assignments; (iii) Local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality due to saddle-dense objectives where additional steps yield diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: The findings inform practical uses of CLS for SAT on modern accelerator hardware, suggesting CLS is best used as a sub-solver in hybrid settings rather than a standalone approach, and that redundant constraints should be avoided.

title: The Capacity of Information-Theoretic Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning
summary: Secure aggregation allows a server to aggregate users' local updates while preserving update privacy. Existing information-theoretic problems typically assume that correlated random keys are provided by a trusted third party (TTP) or generated via prescribed groupwise structures, while the communication cost for establishing such correlated keys is often ignored. Consequently, the fundamental limits under general key-distribution mechanisms remain unknown. In this paper, we study the $T$-colluding information-theoretic secure aggregation problem with $N$ users under a general two-phase framework consisting of a key distribution phase and an update aggregation phase. Unlike prior work, we model key distribution through user-to-user communication and allow arbitrary user-generated key-distribution mechanisms, eliminating TTP or prescribed structures. This enables a joint characterization of three resources: randomness for security, key-distribution communication, and aggregation communication. We completely characterize the capacity region among these three resources by constructing a novel secure aggregation scheme together with a matching information-theoretic converse. In particular, we develop an explicit deterministic capacity-achieving construction over any finite field of size at least $N$, whereas most existing schemes either rely on TTP or employ randomized or existential constructions over sufficiently large finite fields. We further show that the optimal performance can be achieved using only pairwise shared keys, enabling implementation via Diffie--Hellman key exchange. Compared with Google's seminal secure aggregation scheme, the proposed scheme requires fewer random masking keys while preserving the same aggregation communication overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the fundamental capacity region for T-colluding information-theoretic secure aggregation under a general two-phase framework (key distribution + update aggregation), eliminating the need for a trusted third party or prescribed key structures. It achieves a deterministic capacity-achieving scheme over finite fields of size ≥ N, using only pairwise shared keys, and matches the information-theoretic converse.
AI.motivation: Prior work on secure aggregation often assumes correlated random keys from a trusted third party (TTP) or via prescribed groupwise structures, ignoring the communication cost of key distribution. This leaves fundamental limits under general key-distribution mechanisms unknown.
AI.method: The authors model key distribution through user-to-user communication with arbitrary user-generated key-distribution mechanisms. They jointly characterize three resources (randomness, key-distribution communication, aggregation communication) and develop an explicit deterministic capacity-achieving scheme over finite fields of size at least N, with a matching information-theoretic converse.
AI.result: Complete characterization of the capacity region among randomness, key-distribution communication, and aggregation communication. The proposed scheme is deterministic over finite fields of size ≥ N (vs. randomized/existential constructions), requires fewer random masking keys than Google's scheme, and can be implemented via Diffie-Hellman key exchange using only pairwise shared keys.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes fundamental limits for T-colluding secure aggregation without TTP, showing optimal performance is achievable with pairwise shared keys and less randomness than prior art, enabling practical implementation.

title: Multi-Robot Planning and Control from CCTV Camera Networks in a Real Warehouse
summary: Off-board control of mobile robots from cameras embedded in the environment offers a practical path to scalable autonomy, moving sensing and compute off the robots. We extend this idea from the single-robot case to coordinated fleets in a real warehouse, driving multiple robots with only a distributed CCTV network and edge compute. The system operates entirely in image space over an uncalibrated, pixel-wise topological camera graph, enabling wide-area operation with flexible camera placement. A hierarchical planner selects a camera sequence per robot and plans its image-space motion through each view, coordinating robots with a prioritised-then-joint strategy and treating overlapping camera regions as shared resources held by one robot at a time to prevent collisions and deadlocks. We validate the approach in a real warehouse with four robots and 30 cameras across six 27 m aisles, reporting mission times and coordination statistics. To our knowledge, this is the first field demonstration of multi-robot planning and coordination using only an external camera network and off-board compute, with robots carrying no task-specific navigation hardware.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates the first real-world multi-robot fleet coordination using only an external CCTV camera network and edge compute, with robots carrying no onboard navigation hardware. The system uses a hierarchical planner operating in image-space over an uncalibrated topological camera graph to plan and coordinate robot motion, validated in a warehouse with 4 robots and 30 cameras across 6 aisles.
AI.motivation: To extend off-board control from single-robot to coordinated multi-robot fleets in real warehouse environments, removing the need for task-specific navigation hardware on robots and enabling scalable autonomy through distributed camera networks and edge computing.
AI.method: A hierarchical planner operates over an uncalibrated, pixel-wise topological camera graph. It selects a camera sequence per robot and plans image-space motion through each view. Coordination uses a prioritised-then-joint strategy, treating overlapping camera regions as shared resources held by one robot at a time to prevent collisions and deadlocks.
AI.result: Validated in a real warehouse with 4 robots and 30 cameras across six 27m aisles, reporting mission times and coordination statistics. First field demonstration of multi-robot planning and coordination using only an external camera network and off-board compute.
AI.conclusion: Off-board multi-robot coordination using only distributed CCTV networks and edge compute is feasible in real warehouse settings, offering a scalable path to autonomy without requiring robots to carry specialized navigation hardware.

title: JA-SIREN: Deterministic Initialization for Sinusoidal Networks via Spectral Matching
summary: Existing implicit neural representation (INR) approaches suffer from stochastic initialization that does not guarantee consistent or high-quality performance across runs, with variations reaching more than 2.5 dB (78%) in image regression. This variation is problematic for scientific computing and simulation, where result reproducibility is crucial. To address this problem, we present Jacobi-Anger Sinusoidal Representation Network (JA-SIREN), a deterministic initialization scheme for sinusoidal networks grounded in classical spectral analysis. By computing the Discrete Sine Transform (DST) of the target signal and leveraging the Jacobi-Anger expansion, we derive closed-form weights for a two-layer sinusoidal MLP that analytically match the network's initial spectral response to the target signal, requiring no random seed or additional hyperparameter tuning. On the Kodak dataset, JA-SIREN achieves a mean PSNR of 67.18 dB, a 21.30 dB improvement over the best baseline. This is achieved with zero run-to-run variance, confirming that spectrally-informed initialization is a more effective and reproducible alternative to stochastic initialization for sinusoidal INRs.

AI.tldr: JA-SIREN proposes a deterministic initialization scheme for sinusoidal neural networks using DST and Jacobi-Anger expansion, achieving 0 variance and 67.18 dB PSNR on Kodak (21.30 dB improvement over baselines).
AI.motivation: Existing INR approaches suffer from stochastic initialization causing inconsistent performance (up to 2.5 dB / 78% variation in image regression), which is problematic for reproducibility-critical applications like scientific computing and simulation.
AI.method: The method computes the Discrete Sine Transform (DST) of the target signal and uses the Jacobi-Anger expansion to derive closed-form weights for a two-layer sinusoidal MLP, analytically matching the network's initial spectral response to the target signal without random seeds or hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: On the Kodak dataset, JA-SIREN achieves a mean PSNR of 67.18 dB, a 21.30 dB improvement over the best baseline, with zero run-to-run variance.
AI.conclusion: Spectrally-informed initialization is a more effective and reproducible alternative to stochastic initialization for sinusoidal INRs.

title: Principles and Practice of Deep Representation Learning: or a Mathematical Theory of Memory
comment: version 2; TeX source and supplementary material at https://ma-lab-berkeley.github.io/deep-representation-learning-book/
summary:
In the current era of deep learning and especially generative models, there is significant investment in training very large generative models. Thus far, such models have been "black boxes" that are difficult to understand in the sense that they have opaque internal mechanisms, leading to difficulties in interpretability, reliability, and control. Naturally, this lack of understanding has led to both hype and fear.
  This book is an attempt to "open the black box" and understand the mechanisms of large deep networks, through the perspective of representation learning, which is a major factor - arguably the single most important one - in the empirical power of deep learning models. A brief outline of this book is as follows. Chapter 1 will summarize the threads that underlie the whole text. Chapters 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 will explain the design principles of modern neural network architectures through optimization and information theory, reducing the process of architecture development (long having been described as a sort of "alchemy") to undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus exercises once the underlying principles are introduced. Chapters 7 and 8 will discuss applications of these principles to solve problems in more paradigmatic ways, obtaining new methods and models which are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, and yet no less - sometimes even more - powerful than the black-box models they resemble. Chapter 9 will discuss potential future directions for deep learning, the role of representation learning, as well as some open problems.

AI.tldr: This book aims to demystify large deep neural networks by explaining their internal mechanisms through representation learning, using optimization and information theory to transform neural architecture design from "alchemy" into understandable linear algebra and calculus principles.
AI.motivation: Large generative models are "black boxes" with opaque internal mechanisms, leading to difficulties in interpretability, reliability, and control. The authors aim to open this black box to reduce hype and fear stemming from lack of understanding.
AI.method: The book explains modern neural network design principles through optimization and information theory, reducing architecture development to undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus. It covers design principles (Chapters 2-6) and applications (Chapters 7-8) for building efficient, interpretable, and controllable models.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields new methods and models that are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, while being no less (sometimes more) powerful than black-box models.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning is the single most important factor in deep learning's empirical power. The book opens the black box of neural networks and discusses future directions for deep learning and open problems (Chapter 9).

title: RSMA Enabled Hierarchical UAV Networks with Non Linear Energy Harvesting: Outage Probability Analysis and UAV Placement Optimization
comment: Accepted in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are expected to enhance connectivity, extend network coverage, and support advanced communication services in sixth-generation (6G) cellular networks, particularly in public and civil applications. Although multi-UAV systems offer greater efficiency and cost-effectiveness than single-UAV deployments, their implementation still faces several fundamental challenges that limit their reliability, sustainability, and scalability. The limited onboard energy restricts mission duration and communication continuity. Therefore, wireless energy harvesting (EH) emerges as a promising solution to overcome this limitation. However, terrestrial energy sources experience path loss, making EH from surrounding UAVs more sustainable. Moreover, rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) remains insufficiently explored in hierarchical UAV networks under hardware impairments (HWI) and imperfect channel state information (ICSI). This paper proposes a hierarchical ad hoc UAV network with non-linear EH and RSMA to enhance both energy and cost efficiency, where UAVs harvest energy from surrounding UAVs. For a practical scenario, we consider the effect of HWI and ICSI in our proposed system. To the best of the authors knowledge, this study is the first to investigate such a scenario in the literature. The outage probability expressions for ground Internet of things (IoT) devices, each CMU, and the overall outage probability of the proposed system are derived over Nakagami-$m$ fading channels while considering practical constraints such as HWI, ICSI, and non-linear EH. Additionally, approximate outage probability expressions are derived for high transmit power regimes. Subsequently, we formulate two optimization problems to enhance reliability and performance. Our findings indicate that the proposed system outperforms all benchmarks in terms of outage probability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hierarchical ad hoc UAV network using non-linear energy harvesting and rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) to improve energy and cost efficiency in 6G cellular networks. It derives outage probability expressions for ground IoT devices and UAVs under practical constraints like hardware impairments and imperfect channel state information, showing superior performance over benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Multi-UAV systems face reliability, sustainability, and scalability challenges due to limited onboard energy. Wireless energy harvesting is promising but terrestrial sources suffer path loss; harvesting from surrounding UAVs is more sustainable. RSMA remains underexplored in hierarchical UAV networks under practical impairments.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical ad hoc UAV network with non-linear EH and RSMA. Derives exact and approximate outage probability expressions over Nakagami-m fading channels under HWI, ICSI, and non-linear EH. Formulates two optimization problems to enhance reliability and performance.
AI.result: The proposed system outperforms all benchmarks in terms of outage probability. Exact and approximate outage expressions are derived for ground IoT devices, each CMU, and overall system.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical UAV network with non-linear EH and RSMA is an effective solution for enhancing energy efficiency and reliability in 6G networks, particularly under realistic impairments.

title: Impact of Synthetic Lesional MR Images in Automated Focal Cortical Dysplasia Detection in Low-Data Scenarios
summary:
Background and Purpose: Automated detection of focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) requires large volumes of voxelwise lesion-delineated MRI data, which are difficult to acquire. This study aims to generate synthetic MRI data exhibiting FCD, assess their realism, and evaluate their impact on automated FCD detection, particularly in reducing the need for manual annotations.
  Methods: T1-weighted (T1w) and T2-weighted Fluid-Attenuated Inversion Recovery (FLAIR) MRI scans from 131 FCD patients and 90 healthy controls from multiple (3) sites were retrospectively studied. Synthetic MRIs were generated by conditioning a generative network on binary FCD masks. Two neuroradiologists identified real images from a random set of 14 real and 14 synthetic scans. Three nnU-Net models were trained to detect FCD using: (i) real-only (35 FCD / 35 controls), (ii) real (35 FCD / 35 controls) plus synthetic augmentation, and (iii) expanded real data (70 FCD / 70 controls).
  Results: Experts showed limited ability to distinguish real from synthetic images, with classification accuracy of 60% for T1w and 70% for FLAIR (inter-rater agreement kappa = 0.86). Augmenting automated FCD detection with synthetic data increased sensitivity by 8.14% (p = 0.12) and improved model confidence at true lesion sites (0.83 +/- 0.11 to 0.89 +/- 0.12; p = 0.02). The expanded real-data model further improved sensitivity to 73.8% (p < 0.001) and confidence to 0.90 +/- 0.14 (p = 0.01).
  Conclusion: Conditional generative networks can generate realistic synthetic FCD-MRIs, reducing labeled data needs by approximately 20% while maintaining equivalent sensitivity. Equivalent amounts of real data, when available, remain more effective than synthetic augmentation.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that conditional generative networks can create realistic synthetic FCD MRI scans that fool expert radiologists (60-70% accuracy), and using these synthetic images for training reduces the need for manually annotated data by ~20% while maintaining equivalent detection sensitivity, though real data remains more effective when available.
AI.motivation: Automated detection of focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) requires large volumes of voxelwise lesion-delineated MRI data, which are difficult to acquire. The authors aim to generate synthetic MRI data exhibiting FCD to reduce the need for manual annotations.
AI.method: T1w and FLAIR MRI scans from 131 FCD patients and 90 healthy controls were retrospectively studied. Synthetic MRIs were generated by conditioning a generative network on binary FCD masks. Two neuroradiologists evaluated realism. Three nnU-Net models were trained: (i) real-only (35 FCD/35 controls), (ii) real + synthetic augmentation, and (iii) expanded real data (70 FCD/70 controls).
AI.result: Experts classified real vs. synthetic with 60% (T1w) and 70% (FLAIR) accuracy (kappa=0.86). Synthetic augmentation increased sensitivity by 8.14% (p=0.12) and improved model confidence at true lesion sites from 0.83±0.11 to 0.89±0.12 (p=0.02). Expanded real-data model achieved 73.8% sensitivity (p<0.001) and confidence of 0.90±0.14 (p=0.01).
AI.conclusion: Conditional generative networks can generate realistic synthetic FCD-MRIs, reducing labeled data needs by approximately 20% while maintaining equivalent sensitivity. However, equivalent amounts of real data remain more effective than synthetic augmentation.

title: AEGIS: A Backup Reflex for Physical AI
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation tends to fail gradually: one bad step degrades the state, and the policy spirals into a basin from which it cannot recover. The failure is often visible before it happens. We introduce AEGIS (Activation-probe Early-warning, Gated Inference Switching), a selective escalation method that uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps while there is still time to act. When the probe flags a step, control switches to a stronger separate policy, but only for the steps that need it. On LIBERO-Spatial, AEGIS recovers 10.1% of the trajectories the weak policy alone loses, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for a random-trigger placebo. These gains are significant under one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment over three pre-registered contrasts: +5.4pp over blind escalation, p=8.5e-6; +5.0pp over random triggering, p=1.0e-4; paired-trajectory bootstrap CIs exclude zero. AEGIS activates the stronger policy on only 38% of steps, so the lever is timing rather than compute. The probe clears its precondition with an early-window AUROC of 0.764, 95% CI [0.70, 0.84], read from the weak-policy path over the first 30% of trajectory steps before any handoff. We pre-register the full analysis plan, including a conditional recovered-task-rate estimand and explicit kill criteria, and confirm the result on 700 common-random-number episodes per arm, with nA-fail=646.

AI.tldr: AEGIS selectively escalates from a weak to a strong policy in long-horizon robot manipulation by using a lightweight probe to detect high-risk steps early, recovering 10.1% of lost trajectories vs. 4.6% (blind escalation) and 5.1% (random trigger), while activating the stronger policy on only 38% of steps.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robot manipulation tends to fail gradually where one bad step degrades the state irrecoverably, and failure is often visible before it happens, motivating a need for early detection and selective intervention.
AI.method: AEGIS uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps early, switching control to a stronger policy only when needed, based on a pre-registered analysis plan with conditional recovered-task-rate estimand.
AI.result: On LIBERO-Spatial, AEGIS recovers 10.1% of trajectories lost by the weak policy vs. 4.6% (blind escalation) and 5.1% (random trigger). Gains are significant under paired McNemar tests (p=8.5e-6 and p=1.0e-4). The probe achieves early-window AUROC of 0.764 [0.70, 0.84] on the first 30% of steps.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS demonstrates that selective, early-warning-based escalation can significantly improve long-horizon robot manipulation success with minimal compute overhead (stronger policy only on 38% of steps), validated on 700 episodes per arm with pre-registered analysis.

title: Letting Homogeneity Entropy Select S-Pairs in Buchberger's Algorithm
summary: We present a novel S-pair selection strategy called Homogeneity Entropy, for deciding the sequence of S-polynomials to construct in Buchberger's algorithm to compute a Groebner basis. The strategy uses an information theoretic measure derived from the distribution of degrees among the monomials of the S-polynomial: a very different approach to the classical heuristics such as Degree, Normal and Sugar, or indeed the more recent machine learning approaches to the problem. We implement this strategy and evaluate it on two different datasets: (1) variations of randomly generated polynomial systems with controlled numbers of variables, degrees, and densities; and (2) the PHCpack benchmark dataset sourced from real world problems. The Homogeneity Entropy strategy significantly outperforms classical strategies on random polynomial datasets, but on the PHCpack dataset the classical strategies perform better. This suggests the right strategy varies with the shape of the data and we explore this in several experiments. The new strategy offers practically meaningful gains on certain distributions, and represents the first use of such information-theoretic guidance in the optimisation of symbolic computation algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Homogeneity Entropy, an information-theoretic S-pair selection strategy for Buchberger's algorithm that outperforms classical methods on random polynomial systems but underperforms on real-world problems, suggesting dataset-dependent optimal strategies.
AI.motivation: To improve Buchberger's algorithm for computing Gröbner bases by finding a better S-pair selection strategy, moving beyond classical heuristics and recent ML approaches to use information theory.
AI.method: Proposes Homogeneity Entropy, an information-theoretic measure based on the distribution of degrees among monomials in S-polynomials, implemented and tested on random polynomial systems and the PHCpack benchmark dataset.
AI.result: Homogeneity Entropy significantly outperforms classical strategies on random polynomial datasets, but classical strategies perform better on the PHCpack real-world dataset.
AI.conclusion: The optimal S-pair selection strategy is data-dependent; the proposed information-theoretic approach offers meaningful gains on certain distributions and is the first use of such guidance in symbolic computation optimization.

title: Learning All-Terrain Locomotion for a Planetary Rover with Actively Articulated Suspension
summary: This paper presents ERNEST, a four-wheeled planetary rover concept equipped with a two-degree-of-freedom Active Gimbal Suspension that combines yaw and roll actuation to enable wheel reconfiguration, steering, and active load redistribution. A single neural network controller, trained to track a desired path across challenging terrain, fully unlocks the capabilities of this actuated suspension system for autonomous obstacle negotiation. A reinforcement learning framework is developed using the high-fidelity DARTS simulation engine, which combines rigid-contact dynamics and Bekker-Wong terramechanics, enabling the emergence of locomotion strategies adapted to loose-soil conditions. To obtain a single unified controller across heterogeneous terrains, a policy consolidation strategy merges the experience of terrain-specialized agents into one neural network, eliminating the need for explicit terrain classification and controller switching. The resulting controller operates on a combination of proprioceptive and exteroceptive feedback, including sparse stereo-derived terrain elevation, chassis attitude, joint states, and force-torque measurements. Zero-shot transfer to the physical rover is achieved through domain randomization, sensor noise injection, and model-to-real system identification. Experimental results demonstrate autonomous traversal of rock fields, a bump trap, a wheel-high step, sand ripples, and sandy slopes. On a 20° sandy slope, the learned controller reduces the cost of transport by 37% on dry sand despite the additional actuation, and achieves superior performance on wet sand where the passive suspension becomes completely immobilized.

AI.tldr: ERNEST is a four-wheeled rover with an active gimbal suspension that uses reinforcement learning to train a single neural network controller for autonomous navigation over challenging heterogeneous terrains, achieving zero-shot transfer to a physical rover and outperforming passive suspensions on sandy slopes.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous planetary rovers to negotiate challenging heterogeneous terrain (rock fields, steep slopes, loose sand) without requiring explicit terrain classification or controller switching, overcoming the limitations of passive suspension systems that can become immobilized.
AI.method: A four-wheeled rover with 2-DOF Active Gimbal Suspension (yaw+roll); single neural network controller trained via reinforcement learning using DARTS simulation engine (rigid-contact dynamics + Bekker-Wong terramechanics); policy consolidation merging terrain-specialized agents; zero-shot sim-to-real transfer via domain randomization, sensor noise injection, and system identification.
AI.result: Autonomous traversal of rock fields, bump trap, wheel-high step, sand ripples, and sandy slopes. On dry sand 20° slope: 37% reduction in cost of transport despite additional actuation. On wet sand: superior performance where passive suspension became immobilized.
AI.conclusion: A single learned controller with active suspension can generalize across diverse terrains without explicit classification, reduce energy cost on loose soil, and enable traversal where passive systems fail, demonstrating viable zero-shot transfer from simulation to physical hardware.

title: Adaptive Band Selection for Hyperspectral Classification with Spatially Disjoint Evaluation
summary: Hyperspectral band selection methods based on differentiable selectors can be sensitive to initialization and to extracting a final discrete subset, while prescribed band counts limit flexibility. We propose SGBR-HC (Spectral-Group Band Ranking with Hard-Concrete initialization), a two-stage method that uses a supervised spectral ranking to initialize trainable sparse gates rather than treating ranking as a fixed selection rule, letting the number of selected bands be determined by training. Stage-1 scores candidate bands from training pixels by class discriminability and spectral diversity; this ranking seeds the gate logits for Stage-2, which trains the sparse gates jointly with a spatial classifier. Under spatially disjoint evaluation on Pavia University and Houston 2013, verified by retraining a fresh classifier on the selected bands, SGBR-HC achieves the highest mean overall accuracy and Cohen's kappa with approximately twenty bands. Bypassing Stage-1 degrades OA by 8.84 pp on Pavia University and 22.15 pp on Houston 2013, confirming the ranking prior's role. Random pixel splits inflate OA on Pavia University by 30.56 pp, underscoring spatial leakage as a critical evaluation confound.

AI.tldr: SGBR-HC is a two-stage hyperspectral band selection method that uses supervised spectral ranking to initialize trainable sparse gates, allowing the number of selected bands to be learned automatically, achieving high accuracy with ~20 bands under spatially-disjoint evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing differentiable band selection methods are sensitive to initialization and require pre-specified band counts, limiting flexibility and robustness.
AI.method: Stage-1 ranks candidate bands by class discriminability and spectral diversity to seed gate logits; Stage-2 trains sparse gates jointly with a spatial classifier using hard-concrete initialization.
AI.result: Achieves highest mean OA and Cohen's kappa with ~20 bands on Pavia University and Houston 2013; skipping Stage-1 degrades OA by 8.84-22.15 pp; random pixel splits inflate OA by 30.56 pp.
AI.conclusion: The ranking prior is critical for performance, and spatially-disjoint evaluation is essential to avoid overestimation from spatial leakage.

title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope
summary:
Objective. EEG foundation models (FMs) report strong accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG. However, high accuracy under subject-disjoint cross-validation remains ambiguous: it can reflect a genuine clinical biomarker, or subject-identity features that correlate with the label. We name this the Identity Trap and ask whether it can be diagnosed at the representation level before fine-tuning.
  Approach. We propose FMScope, a frozen-representation protocol packaging five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. We apply it to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout: subject relation of label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker.
  Main results. (i) The Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null in 12/12 pairs, rising in all 12 under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves label decoding where the label varies within subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one subject carrier: removing it drops the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
  Significance. The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component, and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope separates gains reflecting a biological marker from those reflecting subject identity.

AI.tldr: EEG foundation models often learn subject-identity features instead of genuine clinical biomarkers, a problem called the "Identity Trap." The authors propose FMScope, a diagnostic protocol, and show this trap is universal across models and datasets, but can be detected and partially mitigated.
AI.motivation: High accuracy in subject-disjoint cross-validation for EEG foundation models is ambiguous—it may reflect genuine clinical biomarkers or subject-identity features correlated with labels. The authors aim to diagnose whether representations capture identity rather than disease-relevant signals before fine-tuning.
AI.method: FMScope: a frozen-representation protocol with five diagnostics—variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. Applied to three FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 design (subject relation of label × presence of consensus cross-subject EEG marker).
AI.result: (i) Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x random null in all 12 model-dataset pairs, worsening with fine-tuning (+10 to +63pp). Erasing subject axis improves label decoding where labels vary within subject (+6 to +12pp primary; +4 to +27pp external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is a subject carrier for LaBraM and CBraMod (removal drops subject probe 9-19pp), but not REVE. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only when a cross-subject biological marker exists.
AI.conclusion: The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded shortcut learning problem with measurable physiological components; subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope enables separating genuine biomarker gains from subject-identity artifacts.

title: Implementation and Calibration of 3GPP-Compliant ISAC Channel Simulator
comment: 6 pages, Codes and other source files are open on GitHub
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a key technology for 6G systems. To support the development of ISAC systems, accurate channel modeling and simulation for performance evaluation is essential. Recently, 3GPP introduced a standardized ISAC channel model and its associated calibration procedure for this purpose. However, due to the complexity of the modeling methodology and the lack of fully explicit implementation details in the 3GPP reports, different implementations may lead to inconsistent or unsynchronized simulation results. To address this issue, in this work, we implement the 3GPP ISAC channel model simulator specified in TR 38.901 and conduct a comprehensive calibration analysis. We compare the simulation results with the reference results reported by companies in 3GPP and discuss several key implementation details to provide insights into the implementation and calibration of the simulator. To facilitate reproducibility and further research, the developed simulator, together with the relevant datasets and calibration results, has been released as an open-source project on GitHub.

AI.tldr: This paper implements the 3GPP ISAC channel model simulator specified in TR 38.901, conducts calibration analysis against reference results, and releases the simulator as open-source to ensure reproducible and synchronized simulations for 6G ISAC system development.
AI.motivation: To support 6G ISAC system development by addressing the lack of explicit implementation details in 3GPP reports, which causes inconsistent simulation results across different implementations.
AI.method: Implemented the 3GPP ISAC channel model simulator specified in TR 38.901 and conducted comprehensive calibration analysis by comparing simulation results with reference results from companies in 3GPP.
AI.result: The simulator produces results consistent with 3GPP reference results; key implementation details are discussed; the simulator, datasets, and calibration results are released as open-source on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The open-source simulator enables reproducible and synchronized evaluations for ISAC systems, bridging gaps in 3GPP documentation and facilitating further research.

title: EvoGS: Constructing Continuous-Layered Gaussian Splatting with Evolution Tree for Scalable 3D Streaming
summary: Streaming 3D Gaussian Splatting requires highly scalable, progressive representations. Existing progressive methods rely on \textit{discrete layering}, accumulating separate splat sets for each level of detail. This structural independence between layers inherently leads to error accumulation, severe splat redundancy, and uncontrolled quality transitions. We propose EvoGS, the first \textit{continuous-layering} representation. Organized as an Evolution Tree, EvoGS generates finer details via an explicit, wavelet-inspired parent-child refinement. This empowers child nodes to structurally correct ancestral errors, yield inherently sparse and highly compressible inter-layer signals. Extensive experiments show EvoGS eliminates splat redundancy from over 65\% to under 25\%. Compared to state-of-the-art baselines, it reduces transmission payload and GPU VRAM footprint by up to 2.4$\times$ and 5.5$\times$, respectively, and achieves smooth quality transitions optimal for real-time adaptive streaming. Project page: https://yuang-ian.github.io/evogs/

AI.tldr: EvoGS introduces a continuous-layering representation (Evolution Tree) for streaming 3D Gaussian Splatting, replacing discrete layering to reduce redundancy, correct ancestral errors, and achieve superior compression and smooth quality transitions.
AI.motivation: Existing progressive 3D Gaussian Splatting methods use discrete layering with independent splat sets, causing error accumulation, splat redundancy, and uncontrolled quality transitions.
AI.method: Proposes EvoGS with an Evolution Tree continuous-layering representation, using wavelet-inspired parent-child refinement where child nodes structurally correct errors from ancestors.
AI.result: Reduces splat redundancy from over 65% to under 25%; reduces transmission payload by up to 2.4× and GPU VRAM footprint by up to 5.5× vs. state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: EvoGS achieves inherently sparse, compressible inter-layer signals with smooth quality transitions optimal for real-time adaptive streaming.

title: A Geometric Account of Activation Steering through Angle-Norm Decomposition
summary: Linear activation steering has gained popularity as a simple and empirically effective way to control language model behavior. More recently, spherical steering paradigms have been proposed to address limitations of additive interventions, often motivated by the assumption that hidden-state norm does not carry concept-relevant information. In this work, we revisit this assumption through a controlled empirical study designed to disentangle the roles of angular and radial components. We show that steering methods differ mainly in how they couple two geometric effects: changing a token's angular alignment with a concept direction and changing its hidden-state norm. Across seven language models, we find that concepts are represented primarily in angular structure, supporting the motivation for spherical methods, but that norm remains important for the stability and downstream effects of steering. Our results explain why interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently, and suggest that activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components of the intervention, rather than by a single additive coefficient that entangles these two effects.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how linear vs. spherical activation steering methods work differently by disentangling angular (direction) and radial (norm) components of hidden states in language models, finding concepts are encoded primarily in angular structure but norm affects stability.
AI.motivation: To resolve whether spherical steering methods outperform linear methods, and to test the assumption that hidden-state norm doesn't carry concept-relevant information, by systematically disentangling angular and radial components of steering interventions.
AI.method: Controlled empirical study across seven language models, disentangling the roles of angular alignment and hidden-state norm changes in steering interventions, comparing linear and spherical paradigms.
AI.result: Concepts are primarily represented in angular structure (supporting spherical methods), but norm remains important for stability and downstream effects. Different steering methods couple these two geometric effects differently.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components separately, rather than a single additive coefficient that entangles these two effects.

title: Rate Loss in Quantum Channels with Classical State and Applications for Quantum Broadcast Channels
summary: We consider the problem of \textit{rate loss} - a strict penalty suffered in achievable rates due to the lack of channel state information at the receiver (Rx) of a classical-quantum (CQ) channel. First, we identify non-commutative CQ channels and analytically prove a rate loss. Building on this, we next prove that coset-code-based strategies can strictly outperform conventional unstructured IID-code-based strategies for non-commutative 3-user CQ broadcast channels.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies rate loss in non-commutative classical-quantum channels due to lack of receiver channel state information, and proves coset codes outperform unstructured IID codes for 3-user CQ broadcast channels.
AI.motivation: To study the rate loss penalty in classical-quantum channels caused by missing channel state information at the receiver, and to determine if structured coding (coset codes) can improve performance over conventional unstructured codes.
AI.method: Analytical proof of rate loss for non-commutative CQ channels, followed by comparative analysis showing coset-code-based strategies outperform IID-code-based strategies for 3-user CQ broadcast channels.
AI.result: Rate loss is proven for non-commutative CQ channels. Coset-code strategies achieve strictly better rates than unstructured IID-code strategies for 3-user non-commutative CQ broadcast channels.
AI.conclusion: Structured coding (coset codes) can mitigate rate loss in non-commutative CQ channels and outperform traditional unstructured coding approaches in multi-user settings.

title: Lane Change Trajectory Planning for Personalized Driving Comfort and Mobility Efficiency
comment: Accepted by the IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IEEE IV 2026), Detroit, MI, United States, June 22_25, 2026
summary: Lane changing entails simultaneous longitudinal and lateral motions that affect driving comfort and mobility efficiency. Because these motions are tightly coupled and subject to substantial inter-vehicle variability, trajectory planning for lane-change maneuvers is characterized by a highly personalized nature. This study proposes a neural network-driven planner that integrates a third-order polynomial trajectory generator with a learning module that infers optimal trajectory parameters across diverse driving conditions. Using a shared backbone with dual heads, one head ensures all-condition operational guarantees, while the other captures driver-specific preferences for comfort or mobility efficiency. A head-gated switching mechanism, realized through a statistical gate based on error-winner logistic regression, adaptively selects the appropriate head under varying driving conditions, which enables context-aware lane-change trajectory planning. Representative cases and Monte Carlo simulations show that the proposed planner achieves personalized comfort and mobility during lane changes, while the baseline ensures feasible trajectories under driving conditions where personalized data are insufficient or inaccessible.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a neural network-based lane-change trajectory planner that uses a dual-head architecture with a gating mechanism to balance personalized driving preferences (comfort vs. mobility) with all-condition operational safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: Lane changing involves coupled longitudinal and lateral motions with high inter-vehicle variability, making trajectory planning highly personalized. Existing methods lack the ability to adapt to individual driver preferences while maintaining safety across diverse conditions.
AI.method: A neural network-driven planner combining a third-order polynomial trajectory generator with a learning module. It features a shared backbone with dual heads (one for all-condition guarantees, one for driver-specific preferences) and a head-gated switching mechanism using error-winner logistic regression for context-aware selection.
AI.result: Representative cases and Monte Carlo simulations demonstrate that the planner achieves personalized comfort and mobility during lane changes, while the baseline ensures feasible trajectories when personalized data is insufficient.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively balances personalization and safety, offering context-aware lane-change trajectory planning adaptable to varying driving conditions and driver preferences.

title: RigPAPR: Rig-Based Animation of Static Neural Point Clouds from a Fixed-Viewpoint Video
comment: An overview video is available at https://youtu.be/up3BwRHYWG8
summary: Static neural point reconstructions capture a subject at high fidelity from posed images. Given such a reconstruction, we aim to animate it to follow a monocular fixed-viewpoint driving video of the subject, whether captured or produced by image-to-video (I2V) generation, and to recover a rigged, re-posable 3D asset. Existing methods deform Gaussian splats through direct linear blend skinning (LBS) or mesh proxies, both of which are prone to joint-boundary artifacts under articulation, even with per-primitive corrections. We trace the artifact to the representation: each splat carries an individual shape calibrated in the canonical pose to tile with its neighbours. Under rigid LBS, each splat moves with its bone but cannot bend, so the canonical tiling breaks at joint boundaries into gaps and spikes. Proximity attention point rendering (PAPR) instead carries no per-primitive shape; each pixel is recomposed at render time from the deformed primitives' positions, so the surface re-forms naturally with the articulation. We present RigPAPR, which auto-rigs a static PAPR cloud and drives it under direct LBS from a single fixed-viewpoint video, without mesh proxy, pose-dependent correction, or category template. On synthetic subjects, RigPAPR matches the strongest baseline at the supervised view and exceeds mesh-based and Gaussian-splatting baselines at novel views by 3+dB PSNR, with cleaner joint-boundary renderings of both synthetic and real subjects.

AI.tldr: RigPAPR animates static neural point reconstructions via direct LBS without joint-boundary artifacts, outperforming baselines in novel-view synthesis.
AI.motivation: Existing methods (Gaussian splats with LBS or mesh proxies) produce joint-boundary artifacts (gaps/spikes) under articulation because each splat has a rigid canonical shape that cannot bend.
AI.method: RigPAPR uses Proximity Attention Point Rendering (PAPR), where per-primitive shape is eliminated and pixels are recomposed from deformed primitive positions, enabling natural surface reformation under LBS without mesh proxies or pose-dependent corrections.
AI.result: On synthetic subjects, RigPAPR matches the strongest baseline at supervised views and exceeds mesh/Gaussian baselines at novel views by 3+dB PSNR, with cleaner joint-boundary renderings on both synthetic and real subjects.
AI.conclusion: PAPR-based representation inherently avoids articulation artifacts, enabling high-quality auto-rigging and animation from a single monocular video without category-specific templates or corrections.

title: Capturing non-Markovian dynamics in non-equilibrium stochastic systems using flow matching
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, Accepted to 2026 Conference on Physics and AI (PAI26)
summary: Hydrodynamic models of stochastic particle systems represented by coarse-grained stochastic partial differential equations (SPDE), such as the regularized Dean-Kawasaki (DK) equation, do not accurately capture the short-time system dynamics that is dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where the distributions are highly non-Gaussian. We develop a generative flow matching method that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations that explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects. As a demonstration, we use this method to simulate the Kramers first passage time problem for a system of non-interacting Brownian particles. We show the model accurately captures the short-time behavior and provides better predictions of the statistical moments of the number density when compared against the solution of the Markovian baseline, regularized DK equation.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a generative flow matching method to model probability distributions of particle fluxes, capturing non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects that standard SPDE models miss, and demonstrates improved accuracy on the Kramers first passage time problem.
AI.motivation: Hydrodynamic models (e.g., regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation) fail to capture short-time non-Markovian dynamics and non-Gaussian distributions in low-density regimes.
AI.method: A generative flow matching approach that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations, explicitly incorporating non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects.
AI.result: The model accurately captures short-time behavior and provides better predictions of statistical moments of number density compared to the regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation.
AI.conclusion: Generative flow matching is an effective alternative to traditional SPDE-based hydrodynamic models for systems with significant non-Markovian and non-Gaussian dynamics.

title: CSI Phase Averaging for High-Sensitivity Wi-Fi Sensing in Low-Multipath Environments
summary: This paper presents a low-complexity motion detection method for outdoor Wi-Fi sensing based on a model-driven approach. The method exploits the structural characteristics of the phase components in channel state information (CSI) for low-multipath propagation environments, which are generally considered disadvantageous for Wi-Fi sensing, to mitigate the phase offset errors originating from wireless devices. In addition, phase averaging provides a processing gain that reduces the random noise components, including quantization and thermal noise. The theoretical basis of the method is described and its effectiveness is experimentally evaluated using Compressed Beamforming frames obtained from commercial IEEE 802.11ac devices. The experiments primarily focus wild crows flying in an outdoor orchard environment. The experimental results demonstrate that the method can detect birds even when they fly several meters away from the direct line-of-sight path between the transmitter and receiver antennas. Furthermore, the results indicated that fluctuations caused by vegetation movement were negligible when the wind speed was less than 3~m/s. The proposed approach is expected to be applicable not only to orchard monitoring but also to other outdoor Wi-Fi sensing applications in low-multipath environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a low-complexity motion detection method for outdoor Wi-Fi sensing using a model-driven approach that leverages CSI phase structural characteristics and phase averaging to mitigate device errors and noise, validated by detecting wild crows in orchards using commercial IEEE 802.11ac devices.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge that outdoor low-multipath environments are generally considered disadvantageous for Wi-Fi sensing, and to mitigate phase offset errors from wireless devices for reliable outdoor motion detection.
AI.method: A model-driven approach exploiting structural characteristics of CSI phase components for low-multipath environments, combined with phase averaging to reduce random noise (quantization and thermal noise).
AI.result: Successfully detected wild crows flying several meters away from the direct line-of-sight path; vegetation movement caused negligible fluctuations when wind speed < 3 m/s.
AI.conclusion: The method is effective for outdoor Wi-Fi sensing in low-multipath environments, applicable to orchard monitoring and other outdoor sensing applications.

title: OpenSkill: Open-World Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures and 8 tables. Code is avalable at https://github.com/OpenLAIR/OpenSkill
summary: Self-evolving agents requires adaptation after deployment, but existing approaches assume a usable learning loop, such as curated skills, successful trajectories, or verifier signals. Real open-world deployments may provide none of these, offering only a task prompt. In this work, we study open-world self-evolution, where an agent must build both its skills and its own verification signals from scratch, using open-world resources but no target-task supervision.
