We propose OpenSkill, a framework that bootstraps this loop: it acquires grounded knowledge and verification anchors from documentation, repositories, and the web, synthesizes them into transferable skills, and refines those skills against self-built virtual tasks grounded in the anchors rather than in target answers. The open world thus supplies both the knowledge to be learned and a supervision-independent practice environment, with target-task supervision reserved for final evaluation. Across three benchmarks and two target agents, OpenSkill attains the best automated pass rate while satisfying the no-supervision constraint. Analysis shows its skills transfer across models without model-specific adaptation, and its self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes despite never accessing them.

AI.tldr: OpenSkill enables agents to self-evolve in open-world settings by building skills and verification signals from scratch using only a task prompt, without any target-task supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing self-evolving agents assume a usable learning loop (curated skills, successful trajectories, verifier signals), but real open-world deployments may lack these, offering only a task prompt.
AI.method: OpenSkill bootstraps self-evolution by: (1) acquiring grounded knowledge and verification anchors from documentation, repositories, and the web, (2) synthesizing them into transferable skills, and (3) refining skills against self-built virtual tasks grounded in those anchors rather than target answers.
AI.result: OpenSkill achieves the best automated pass rate across three benchmarks and two target agents while satisfying the no-supervision constraint. Skills transfer across models without model-specific adaptation, and the self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes despite never accessing them.
AI.conclusion: The open world can supply both the knowledge to be learned and a supervision-independent practice environment, eliminating the need for target-task supervision in agent self-evolution.

title: Sort, Partition, Randomize: Optimal Binary Hypothesis Testing under Local Differential Privacy
summary: We study optimal design of $\varepsilon$-locally differentially private mechanisms for binary hypothesis testing. Each observation is drawn from one of two known distributions $P_0,P_1$ on a finite alphabet of size $k$, privatized by a mechanism $Q$, and then used to infer which distribution generated the data. We measure testing utility using an $f$-divergence, including total variation, KL, and hockey-stick divergences, between the two induced output distributions. Previous work established structural properties of optimal mechanisms, but only yielded exponential-time algorithms. We prove a sharp structure: for every $\varepsilon$ and every $f$-divergence objective, after sorting the alphabet by likelihood ratio, there exists an optimal mechanism that partitions the sorted alphabet into contiguous blocks and applies randomized response to the block label. We call this class Sort-Partition-Randomize (SPR). This characterization yields an exact dynamic program that computes an optimal mechanism in $O(k^3)$ time, and more generally in $O(\ell k^2)$ time with an $\ell$-output budget. Our results make it possible to efficiently compute and characterize the exact optimum across the full privacy range, beyond asymptotic privacy regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper studies optimal locally differentially private mechanisms for binary hypothesis testing, proving that optimal mechanisms have a "Sort-Partition-Randomize" (SPR) structure: sort alphabet by likelihood ratio, partition into contiguous blocks, and apply randomized response to block labels. This enables an exact dynamic programming algorithm in O(k^3) time, making exact optimal mechanism computation feasible across the full privacy range.
AI.motivation: Previous work established structural properties of optimal ε-LDP mechanisms for binary hypothesis testing but only yielded exponential-time algorithms, making them impractical. This paper aims to find a more efficient, exact characterization that enables polynomial-time computation of optimal mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors prove that for every ε and f-divergence objective, after sorting the alphabet by likelihood ratio, there exists an optimal mechanism that partitions the sorted alphabet into contiguous blocks and applies randomized response to the block label (SPR class). They then derive an exact dynamic programming algorithm that computes the optimal mechanism in O(k^3) time, or O(ℓ k^2) time with an ℓ-output budget.
AI.result: The SPR structural characterization is sharp. The dynamic program computes the exact optimal mechanism in O(k^3) time, a dramatic improvement over previous exponential-time algorithms. The approach works across the full privacy range (not just asymptotic regimes) and generalizes to ℓ-output budget scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The paper makes it possible to efficiently compute and characterize the exact optimal locally private mechanism for binary hypothesis testing across all privacy levels, bridging the gap between theoretical structural properties and practical computability.

title: Three-dimensional hydro-cluttered locomotion by an undulatory robot
summary: Aquatic robots have expanded human access to underwater environments, yet many underwater spaces contain obstacles that can disrupt open-water locomotion. In "hydro-cluttered" environments, water is interspersed with rigid and flexible clutter, making body-obstacle contact unavoidable. Operating in these spaces requires robots that can regulate and exploit contact, but this regime remains difficult to model or simulate. Building on recent advances in mechanical intelligence in terradynamically capable limbless robotics, we develop principles for 3D aquatic locomotion using AquaMILR, an elongate limbless robot that combines bilateral cable-driven actuation, programmable body compliance, distributed depth regulation, corrosion-resistant enclosures, and onboard power and electronics for untethered field operation. Systematic robophysical experiments reveal that programmable body compliance regulates body deformation and converts body-environment interactions into fast, robust, forward progression across increasing hydro-clutter constraint strength. Depth regulation provides three-dimensional access, allowing the robot to bypass clutter, recover from obstruction, and continue through otherwise inaccessible routes. In potential jamming scenarios, emergent inertia-induced rolling acts as a spontaneous recovery mechanism, freeing the robot from clutter that would otherwise lead to failure and allowing locomotion to continue without additional control. Tests of the robot in an aquatic mangrove field demonstrate that these principles transfer to practical operation, enabling navigation and onboard visual inspection of inaccessible root zones. These results establish principles for hydro-cluttered locomotion and a design paradigm in which aquatic robots exploit environmental complexity as a locomotor resource.

AI.tldr: This paper presents AquaMILR, an elongate limbless robot that demonstrates how programmable body compliance, depth regulation, and emergent rolling mechanics enable effective locomotion through "hydro-cluttered" underwater environments by exploiting rather than avoiding body-obstacle contact.
AI.motivation: Underwater environments often contain obstacles (rigid and flexible clutter) that disrupt traditional open-water locomotion. Body-obstacle contact is unavoidable in these spaces, yet current robots lack principles for operating in such "hydro-cluttered" regimes, which remain difficult to model or simulate.
AI.method: The authors developed AquaMILR, an untethered, elongate limbless robot with bilateral cable-driven actuation, programmable body compliance, distributed depth regulation, and corrosion-resistant enclosures. They conducted systematic robophysical experiments across varying hydro-clutter constraint strengths and field tests in an aquatic mangrove environment.
AI.result: Programmable body compliance regulates deformation and converts body-environment interactions into fast, robust forward progression. Depth regulation enables 3D access to bypass clutter and recover from obstruction. In jamming scenarios, emergent inertia-induced rolling acts as a spontaneous recovery mechanism without additional control. Field tests confirmed practical navigation and visual inspection of inaccessible mangrove root zones.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes principles for hydro-cluttered locomotion and a design paradigm where aquatic robots exploit environmental complexity as a locomotor resource rather than treating it as an obstacle to be avoided.

title: RPC-GS: Gaussian Splatting with native RPC Rendering for Satellite Imagery
summary: We present RPC-GS, the first Gaussian Splatting framework for satellite imagery that operates natively with Rational Polynomial Camera (RPC) models. The RPC model is the de facto standard for representing the complex imaging geometry of modern pushbroom satellite sensors. To simplify rendering, prior satellite Gaussian Splatting methods replace the RPC model with perspective or affine camera approximations, leading to geometric errors during reconstruction. RPC-GS avoids these approximations by projecting Gaussian means and covariances directly through the RPC model during the splatting process. We embed the RPC model in a chain of carefully selected geo-coordinate transformations representing a mapping from splatting-suitable scene coordinates to image coordinates. To map the Gaussian covariance matrices, we derive a numerically robust Jacobian-based covariance projection for the (partially nonlinear) coordinate transformations. Since RPCs lack an explicit notion of camera depth, we integrate a metric ray-based depth formulation. We benchmark RPC, perspective, and affine camera models in a unified framework, with our native RPC renderer consistently achieving the lowest reconstruction error on leading satellite benchmark datasets, improving mean altitude error over perspective and affine approximations by 29.6% and 63.8% on DFC2019, and by 9.9% and 37.9% on IARPA2016. We release our code to support future research of Gaussian Splatting in the satellite imaging domain.

AI.tldr: RPC-GS is the first Gaussian Splatting framework for satellite imagery that directly uses RPC models without geometric approximations, achieving significantly lower reconstruction errors on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Prior satellite Gaussian Splatting methods replace the standard RPC model with perspective or affine camera approximations, introducing geometric errors during 3D reconstruction. The authors aim to avoid these approximations by natively incorporating RPC models.
AI.method: RPC-GS projects Gaussian means and covariances directly through the RPC model using carefully selected geo-coordinate transformations. It derives a numerically robust Jacobian-based covariance projection for partially nonlinear transformations and integrates a metric ray-based depth formulation since RPCs lack explicit depth.
AI.result: On DFC2019, RPC-GS improves mean altitude error over perspective and affine approximations by 29.6% and 63.8%. On IARPA2016, improvements are 9.9% and 37.9% respectively, consistently achieving the lowest reconstruction error.
AI.conclusion: Native RPC rendering outperforms perspective and affine approximations in satellite Gaussian Splatting, and the authors release code to support future research in this domain.

title: Explainable Runtime Dependency Tracking for AI-RAN Conflict Monitoring
summary:
Future AI-integrated Radio Access Networks (AI-RAN) will combine open programmability with learning-enabled xApps, rApps, and control functions that act on shared parameters and key performance indicators (KPIs). For conflict monitoring, it is not enough to know which applications are deployed; the system must also know whether the parameter--KPI dependencies assumed by runtime diagnosis remain valid under the current operating regime. This paper studies a lightweight monitoring primitive for that purpose: tracking an interpretable dependency representation from streaming telemetry events.
  We represent active dependencies by a Boolean matrix and use Boolean matrix multiplication to check whether recent parameter-activity and KPI-response events are consistent with the current estimate. We propose a sliding-window inference procedure that reuses the estimate when it remains consistent and recomputes it when recent observations indicate structural change. The tracker is intended as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not as an autonomous mitigation mechanism. Experiments on controlled Boolean event streams show efficient and accurate tracking under dependency changes and Boolean observation noise.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a lightweight monitoring primitive for AI-RAN that tracks Boolean matrix representations of parameter-KPI dependencies from streaming telemetry, using sliding-window inference to detect structural changes and flag inconsistencies for conflict diagnosis.
AI.motivation: In AI-integrated RAN, multiple xApps/rApps act on shared parameters and KPIs, and static dependency assumptions may become invalid under changing operating regimes. The system needs a lightweight method to verify whether assumed parameter-KPI dependencies remain valid.
AI.method: The approach represents active dependencies as a Boolean matrix and uses Boolean matrix multiplication to check consistency between parameter-activity events and KPI-response events. A sliding-window inference procedure reuses the estimate when consistent, and recomputes it upon detection of structural change.
AI.result: Experiments on controlled Boolean event streams demonstrate efficient and accurate tracking under dependency changes and Boolean observation noise.
AI.conclusion: The tracker serves as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not as an autonomous mitigation mechanism.

title: Beyond Backscatter: InSAR coherence from detected SAR images
summary: In this work, we propose a deep learning framework for coherence regression directly from detected SAR images, without the need for accurate coregistration. A Residual U-Net is trained using coherence maps derived from precisely coregistered Sentinel-1 SLC data to learn the relationship between backscatter magnitudes and coherence. The model is trained on 12-day SLC pairs and evaluated across different datasets, including coregistered SLC products and open access analysis-ready data, covering diverse radiometric properties, geometries, and locations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high-resolution coherence regression with improved accuracy compared to existing intensity-based approaches. The network generalizes well across diverse geographical locations and even across different temporal baselines that were never seen at training time. Additionally, the ability to operate on globally available analysis-ready data, such as ground range detected data, e.g., distributed through Google Earth Engine, enables its large-scale application in mission design, change monitoring, and diverse mapping tasks.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework (Residual U-Net) regresses coherence directly from SAR images without requiring accurate coregistration, trained on Sentinel-1 data, achieving high-resolution results that generalize across locations and temporal baselines.
AI.motivation: To eliminate the need for accurate coregistration in SAR coherence estimation and enable large-scale coherence regression from globally available analysis-ready data (e.g., Google Earth Engine).
AI.method: A Residual U-Net is trained on precisely coregistered Sentinel-1 SLC data to learn mapping from backscatter magnitudes to coherence maps.
AI.result: Achieves high-resolution coherence regression with improved accuracy over intensity-based methods; generalizes well across diverse locations and unseen temporal baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method enables large-scale coherence applications (mission design, change monitoring, mapping) using globally available analysis-ready data without coregistration.

title: AdMem: Advanced Memory for Task-solving Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise as tool-using agents but remain limited in long-horizon tasks that require remembering, organizing, and reusing knowledge. Prior memory approaches aim to resolve the situation, but mainly focus on storing factual information. Recent work on procedural memory improves task reuse, yet often reduces to replaying past successes without addressing failure cases or online scalability. We introduce a unified and automatic memory framework that integrates semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level design combining short-term and long-term stores. A multi-agent architecture with actor, memory, and critic agents enables automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval. Long-term memory is managed through reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning, ensuring scalability and continual improvement. Experiments across various environments show that our approach improves robustness and success on long multi-turn tasks compared to existing baselines. This work highlights the importance of comprehensive, adaptive memory for advancing LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified automatic memory framework with bi-level (short-term/long-term) storage integrating semantic, episodic, and procedural memory, using a multi-agent architecture (actor, memory, critic) for automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval, showing improved robustness on long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs as tool-using agents struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring remembering, organizing, and reusing knowledge. Prior memory approaches focus on factual storage or replaying past successes without addressing failure cases or online scalability.
AI.method: A unified memory framework integrating semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level design (short-term + long-term stores). A multi-agent architecture with actor, memory, and critic agents enables automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval. Long-term memory uses reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning for scalability.
AI.result: Experiments across various environments show improved robustness and success on long multi-turn tasks compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Comprehensive, adaptive memory is important for advancing LLM-based agents in long-horizon tasks.

title: STRIPS-WM: Learning Grounded Propositional STRIPS-style World Models from Images
summary: Robots performing long-horizon visual manipulation observe high-dimensional images, but successful plans depend on action-relevant facts: what can be done now and what changes afterward. A useful planning representation should discard irrelevant visual details while preserving action applicability and effects. Classical task planners exploit this structure through symbolic operators with preconditions and effects, but obtaining such representations from raw visual experience remains challenging. We study a visual task-planning setting in which a robot receives only image transitions: the current image, executed high-level action, and the resulting image. At test time, given a start image and a goal image, the robot must produce a sequence of high-level actions that reaches the goal. To address this problem, we introduce STRIPS-WM, a framework for learning image-grounded STRIPS-style world models directly from visual transitions. STRIPS-WM first induces a finite abstract transition graph from images, then learns latent binary predicates and one grounded propositional operator per action label. The learned operators form a symbolic action model with sparse preconditions and add/delete effects. Finally, the learned predicates are distilled into a visual encoder, enabling classical planning directly from novel start and goal images. Experiments on visual rearrangement tasks show that STRIPS-WM improves image-to-plan success over the tested visual rollout, latent graph-search and latent-symbolic baselines.

AI.tldr: STRIPS-WM learns symbolic planning operators with preconditions and effects directly from visual transitions (image, action, next image), enabling classical planning from images for long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Robots performing long-horizon visual manipulation observe high-dimensional images, but successful plans depend only on action-relevant facts. A useful planning representation should discard irrelevant visual details while preserving action applicability and effects. Obtaining symbolic representations from raw visual experience remains challenging.
AI.method: STRIPS-WM first induces a finite abstract transition graph from images, then learns latent binary predicates and one grounded propositional operator per action label. The learned operators form a symbolic action model with sparse preconditions and add/delete effects. Finally, predicates are distilled into a visual encoder for classical planning from novel images.
AI.result: On visual rearrangement tasks, STRIPS-WM improves image-to-plan success over tested visual rollout, latent graph-search, and latent-symbolic baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges raw visual experience and symbolic planning by learning STRIPS-style world models, enabling effective long-horizon planning from images.

title: S23DR 2026 Winning Solution
summary: This text presents the winning solution to the S23DR 2026 challenge for structured 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse SfM, fitted depth, and semantic segmentations. The method treats vertices as a conditional set and denoises 64 vertex tokens with a flow-matching DiT conditioned on Perceiver-style scene tokens. A global pass predicts the coarse structure, a hull-cropped second pass refines it, and a small multi-sample consensus step keeps the stochastic sampler well behaved. The final system ranked first on the private leaderboard, achievingHSS = 0.654.

AI.tldr: Winning solution for S23DR 2026 challenge: uses flow-matching DiT with Perceiver-style conditioning, two-pass refinement (coarse global + hull-cropped), and consensus sampling to reconstruct 3D wireframes from sparse inputs, achieving top private leaderboard score (HSS=0.654).
AI.motivation: To achieve high-quality structured 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse Structure-from-Motion (SfM), fitted depth, and semantic segmentations, as demonstrated by winning the S23DR 2026 challenge.
AI.method: Treats vertices as a conditional set, denoising 64 vertex tokens via a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer (DiT) conditioned on Perceiver-style scene tokens. Uses a global pass for coarse structure, then a hull-cropped second pass for refinement, plus a multi-sample consensus step to stabilize the stochastic sampler.
AI.result: Ranked first on the private leaderboard with HSS = 0.654.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively reconstructs structured 3D wireframes from sparse inputs, and the multi-sample consensus step is key for keeping the stochastic sampler well-behaved.

title: Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IEEE CAI). 6 pages, 3 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/USD-AI-ResearchLab/uncertainty-aware-llm-rl
summary: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences remain persistent challenges in Reinforcement Learning (RL), often resulting in slow convergence, weak generalization, and inefficient exploration. We propose Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping (ULPS), a novel framework that integrates a calibrated Large Language Model (LLM) into the RL training loop to provide structured, uncertainty-modulated behavioral guidance. ULPS employs an A*-based oracle to synthesize optimal symbolic trajectories, which are used to fine-tune a BERT-based language model. During training, this model supplies action suggestions whose influence is conditioned on epistemic uncertainty estimated via Monte Carlo (MC) dropout. An entropy-based blending mechanism adaptively balances LLM guidance and the learned policy (via Proximal Policy Optimization, PPO), allowing the agent to prioritize reliable priors while preserving adaptability. We evaluate ULPS on the MiniGridUnlockPickup benchmark and observe consistent improvements in success rate, reward efficiency, and sample complexity over unguided, uncalibrated, and standard RL baselines. ULPS achieves more than 9% improvement in execution accuracy after fine-tuning, requires fewer environment interactions, and yields higher reward AUC. Our results demonstrate that integrating symbolic A* trajectories, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control offers a principled and effective approach to multi-task reinforcement learning in sparse-reward domains, with potential extensibility to partially observable and multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: ULPS integrates a calibrated LLM with uncertainty-aware blending and A*-guided symbolic trajectories to improve RL performance in sparse-reward, multi-task environments, achieving over 9% accuracy improvement on MiniGridUnlockPickup.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences cause slow convergence, weak generalization, and inefficient exploration in RL.
AI.method: ULPS uses an A*-based oracle to generate symbolic trajectories, fine-tunes a BERT-based LLM on them, then incorporates LLM action suggestions modulated by MC dropout epistemic uncertainty and adaptively blended with PPO policy via entropy-based mechanism.
AI.result: Over 9% improvement in execution accuracy, fewer environment interactions, higher reward AUC, and consistent improvements over baselines on MiniGridUnlockPickup.
AI.conclusion: Integrating symbolic A* trajectories, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control is a principled approach for multi-task RL in sparse-reward domains, with potential for POMDP and multi-agent settings.

title: Amortized Neural Optimization for Pre-Layout Signal Integrity Design Space Exploration using Differentiable Surrogates
summary: Pre-layout design space exploration (DSE) for high-speed signal integrity (SI) analysis is often limited by the computational cost of simulations and iterative optimization algorithms within modern electronic design automation (EDA) workflows. While machine learning surrogate models accelerate the simulation step, optimizing designs still requires utilizing iterative black-box search methods. This iterative nature scales poorly, making multi-corner sweeps computationally expensive. As a solution, this paper proposes amortized neural optimization (ANO) for pre-layout SI design. ANO entirely eliminates iterative black-box inference by utilizing fully differentiable neural network surrogate models. ANO extracts analytical gradients from the surrogate to train a global optimization policy. Instead of solving the optimization problem repeatedly at inference, the optimization process is learned offline and therefore amortized. Once the ANO policy is trained, it maps different channel contexts directly to near-optimal design parameters in a single deterministic forward pass. The efficiency and accuracy of the ANO framework are demonstrated based on three complex SI design scenarios, including DDR5 decision feedback equalization (DFE), 9-dimensional SerDes Tx/Rx co-equalization, and DDR3 DQS differential pair routing to optimize eye diagram metrics under intra-pair skew constraints. By trading roughly 10% in optimality compared to instance-specific black-box algorithms, it realizes speedups of three to four orders of magnitude. For a large-scale 320,000-instance multi-corner SerDes sweep optimization, ANO collapses what would have taken days of computation using iterative search algorithms into a single batched forward pass that completes in milliseconds. This transforms computationally expensive SI optimization into real-time and interactive pre-layout DSE.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes amortized neural optimization (ANO) for pre-layout signal integrity design, using fully differentiable neural network surrogates to eliminate iterative black-box search. ANO learns the optimization policy offline and maps channel contexts to near-optimal design parameters in a single forward pass, achieving 3-4 order speedups with roughly 10% optimality trade-off.
AI.motivation: Pre-layout design space exploration for high-speed signal integrity is limited by high computational cost of simulations and iterative optimization algorithms, making multi-corner sweeps prohibitively expensive.
AI.method: The authors propose amortized neural optimization (ANO) which uses fully differentiable neural network surrogate models to extract analytical gradients, enabling training of a global optimization policy offline. At inference, the policy directly maps channel contexts to near-optimal design parameters in a single deterministic forward pass, eliminating iterative black-box search entirely.
AI.result: ANO demonstrates speedups of three to four orders of magnitude across three complex SI scenarios (DDR5 DFE, 9D SerDes Tx/Rx co-equalization, DDR3 DQS routing), with roughly 10% optimality trade-off. For a 320,000-instance multi-corner SerDes sweep, it reduces days of computation to milliseconds.
AI.conclusion: Amortized neural optimization transforms computationally expensive SI optimization into real-time, interactive pre-layout DSE, enabling practical large-scale multi-corner design space exploration that was previously infeasible.

title: Evidence-Based Intelligent Diagnostic and Therapeutic Visualization System with Large Language Models: Multi-Turn Interaction and Multimodal Treatment Plan Generation
comment: 29 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, including supporting information
summary: Aim: Existing AI-assisted traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic tools suffer from opaque reasoning processes, passive interaction, and limited treatment plan presentation. This study proposes a knowledge-enhanced visual diagnostic system to improve the transparency and interpretability of syndrome differentiation and treatment. Methods: The system is built upon a Neo4j knowledge graph comprising 241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, and 2,485 relations. It incorporates a four-stage symptom matching pipeline (exact, semantic, fuzzy, and large language model verification), an information gain-driven proactive questioning strategy optimized with genetic algorithms, and a multimodal treatment presentation integrating artificial intelligence-generated illustrations, three-dimensional meridian-acupoint models, and evidence-based literature. Results: Knowledge graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%. Case studies validated the effectiveness of the interactive workflow across patient self-assessment, clinician-assisted diagnosis, and traditional Chinese medicine education. Automated paired-comparison evaluation across 30 cases further demonstrated significant improvements in diagnostic trust (Cohen's d = 1.82, p < 0.001), reduced cognitive load (improvements in four of five dimensions), and higher credibility of evidence-based references (4.21 vs. 2.95). Conclusions: The proposed system enhances the transparency of traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic reasoning and the interpretability of treatment plans through knowledge graph-driven visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a practical solution for trustworthy artificial intelligence-assisted traditional Chinese medicine applications.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-enhanced visual diagnostic system using Neo4j knowledge graph, multi-stage symptom matching, proactive questioning, and multimodal treatment presentation improves transparency, interpretability, and trust in AI-assisted traditional Chinese medicine diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-assisted TCM diagnostic tools suffer from opaque reasoning, passive interaction, and limited treatment plan presentation, lacking transparency and interpretability.
AI.method: Builds a Neo4j knowledge graph (241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, 2,485 relations); four-stage symptom matching pipeline (exact, semantic, fuzzy, LLM verification); information gain-driven proactive questioning optimized with genetic algorithms; multimodal treatment presentation with AI-generated illustrations, 3D meridian-acupoint models, and evidence-based literature.
AI.result: Knowledge graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%; case studies validated effectiveness; automated evaluation across 30 cases showed significant improvements in diagnostic trust (Cohen's d=1.82, p<0.001), reduced cognitive load (4 of 5 dimensions), and higher credibility of evidence-based references (4.21 vs. 2.95).
AI.conclusion: The system enhances transparency of diagnostic reasoning and interpretability of treatment plans via knowledge graph-driven visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a trustworthy AI-assisted TCM solution.

title: Think Like a Pilot: Fine-Grained Long-Horizon UAV Navigation
summary: Language-guided UAV agents must execute long-horizon semantic instructions while producing smooth, physically feasible continuous flight commands, yet existing Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) benchmarks typically use discrete or coarse actions and existing UAV Vision-Language-Action (VLA) tasks focus on short, atomic maneuvers. To address this gap in UAV task settings, we introduce \textbf{FLIGHT}, a \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{L}ong-horizon \textbf{I}nstruction-\textbf{G}uided benchmark for \textbf{H}ybrid UAV navigation and reasoning \textbf{T}asks, which combines multi-stage instructions with dense 6-DoF trajectory annotations across two dataset splits: Fine-grained VLN and Long-horizon Flow. To endow the UAV agent with the capability of real-time in-flight reasoning over task execution status and mission planning, while simultaneously accommodating high-frequency, real-time precise control, we further propose \textbf{FLIGHT VLA}, an asynchronous architecture that decouples a low-frequency Streaming Pilot Vision-Language Model (VLM) for task-state reasoning from a high-frequency diffusion action model for continuous control, supervised by explicit \textbf{Pilot Reasoning} texts that summarize the current flight state and anticipate the next subgoal. In closed-loop evaluation, FLIGHT VLA consistently surpasses representative VLN and VLA baselines on our FLIGHT benchmarks, achieving stronger multi-stage completion, subgoal adherence, and terminal control. Its trained Streaming Pilot Reasoning VLM further improves UAV video reasoning, validating the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FLIGHT, a benchmark for fine-grained long-horizon language-guided UAV navigation, and FLIGHT VLA, an asynchronous architecture combining a low-frequency streaming pilot VLM for reasoning with a high-frequency diffusion action model for control, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN benchmarks use discrete/coarse actions and existing UAV VLA tasks focus on short atomic maneuvers, creating a gap for long-horizon semantic instructions requiring smooth continuous flight commands.
AI.method: FLIGHT VLA uses an asynchronous architecture that decouples a low-frequency Streaming Pilot VLM for task-state reasoning from a high-frequency diffusion action model for continuous control, supervised by explicit Pilot Reasoning texts.
AI.result: FLIGHT VLA consistently surpasses representative VLN and VLA baselines on the FLIGHT benchmarks, achieving stronger multi-stage completion, subgoal adherence, and terminal control, and improves UAV video reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed asynchronous VLA architecture effectively bridges high-level reasoning and low-level control for long-horizon UAV navigation tasks, validated by benchmark performance and downstream video reasoning improvements.

title: MMBU: A Massive Multi-modal Biomedical Understanding Benchmark to Probe the Perception Capabilities of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision and language models (VLMs) hold immense promise to transform biomedical imaging workflows, from detecting lesions in chest X-rays to profiling cellular features in microscopy. Realizing this potential, however, requires robust and fine-grained visual perception. Models need to correctly interpret subtle features in images, and they must do so across diverse biomedical modalities, scales, and contexts. Nevertheless, current benchmarks remain limited. To address these gaps, we introduce the Massive Multimodal Biomedical Understanding (MMBU) benchmark. It is the largest biomedical vision and language benchmark to date, covering 35 submodalities with rich structured metadata. It includes both open and closed versions of ungrounded classification, grounded classification, and object detection, enabling systematic evaluation of model performance across biological scales, clinical settings, and imaging modalities. Evaluating 15 open-weight and 2 frontier VLMs, we find that while medical adaptation provides measurable gains for some models, the high accuracy often reported on established benchmarks can mask deficiencies in visual perception and domain generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MMBU, the largest biomedical vision-language benchmark to date, covering 35 submodalities across ungrounded classification, grounded classification, and object detection tasks. Evaluation of 17 VLMs reveals that high accuracy on existing benchmarks can mask deficiencies in visual perception and domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing biomedical VL benchmarks are limited in scope, lacking fine-grained visual perception evaluation across diverse modalities, scales, and contexts, which hinders the realization of VLM potential in biomedical imaging.
AI.method: The authors built MMBU, the largest biomedical VL benchmark covering 35 submodalities with structured metadata, including open and closed versions of three task types: ungrounded classification, grounded classification, and object detection.
AI.result: Evaluated 15 open-weight and 2 frontier VLMs. Found that medical adaptation improves some models, but high accuracy on established benchmarks often masks deficiencies in visual perception and domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Current VLM performance on standard biomedical benchmarks is misleading; MMBU reveals significant gaps in fine-grained visual perception and generalization across modalities, suggesting the need for more comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

title: Spatiotemporal Imputation with Graph-Informed Flow Matching
summary: Missing data is a common challenge in spatiotemporal systems, arising in applications such as air quality monitoring and urban traffic management. Traditional machine learning approaches, like recurrent and graph neural networks, rely on iterative propagation, which tends to accumulate errors over time and space. Recent diffusion-based methods mitigate error propagation but require iterative sampling and often depend on problem-agnostic Gaussian priors, limiting both efficiency and effectiveness. To address these limitations, we propose GiFlow, a Graph-Informed Flow Matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation. GiFlow replaces the typical Gaussian prior with a graph-informed prior constructed via spatiotemporal filtering of observable signals, which better aligns the source distribution to the target and thereby simplifies the generation trajectory. The flow field is parameterized by a hybrid vector field model that integrates spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation, enabling joint modeling of spatial and temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed GiFlow outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in spatiotemporal imputation. The code is available at https://github.com/zepengzhang/GiFlow.

AI.tldr: GiFlow proposes a Graph-Informed Flow Matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation, replacing Gaussian priors with graph-informed priors and using a hybrid vector field model, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Missing data is common in spatiotemporal systems. Traditional ML methods (RNNs, GNNs) accumulate errors over time/space; diffusion-based methods require iterative sampling and use problem-agnostic Gaussian priors, limiting efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.method: GiFlow uses a graph-informed prior (via spatiotemporal filtering of observable signals) instead of Gaussian prior, simplifying the generation trajectory. The flow field is parameterized by a hybrid vector field model integrating spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show GiFlow outperforms state-of-the-art spatiotemporal imputation methods.
AI.conclusion: GiFlow effectively addresses limitations of prior methods by combining graph-informed priors with a hybrid vector field, achieving superior spatiotemporal imputation performance.

title: Workflow-to-Skill: Skill Creation via Routing-Workflow-Semantics-Attachments Decomposition
summary: Large language model agents increasingly rely on Skills to encode procedural knowledge, yet high-quality Skills remain costly to hand-write. This paper studies automatic Skill construction from heterogeneous interaction evidence, including demonstrations, agent trajectories, tool traces, and execution logs. We argue that trace-to-skill construction is not simple summarization tasks, because traces are fragmented, redundant, and may miss rare but safety-critical behaviors. To address this, we introduce RWSA, a workflow-oriented intermediate representation that decomposes Skills into Workflow structure, execution Semantics, and runtime Attachments, capturing task decomposition, control flow, verification, safety, rollback, and state management. Building on RWSA, we propose W2S, a framework that segments traces, induces local Skill drafts, aligns shared structures, reconciles branches, and compresses redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations. Experiments on 70 Skills show that W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization- and prompting-based baselines, highlighting the need to treat traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes RWSA (a workflow-oriented intermediate representation) and W2S (a framework for automatic Skill construction from heterogeneous traces like demonstrations and logs), showing 10.5% improvement in behavioral replay consistency over baselines by treating traces as executable specifications rather than compressible text.
AI.motivation: High-quality Skills for LLM agents are costly to hand-write, and automatic construction from heterogeneous traces is non-trivial because traces are fragmented, redundant, and may omit rare but safety-critical behaviors — they cannot be treated as simple summarization tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces RWSA (Workflow structure, execution Semantics, runtime Attachments) as an intermediate representation for decomposing Skills. Then proposes W2S framework that segments traces, induces local Skill drafts, aligns shared structures, reconciles branches, and compresses redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations.
AI.result: Experiments on 70 Skills show W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization- and prompting-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that traces should be treated as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text, highlighting the need for structure-aware Skill construction approaches.

title: What Is My Robot Thinking? Design Considerations for Transparent and Trustworthy Shared Autonomy
comment: 9 pages, 5 Figures, Code and videos are available at https://sites.google.com/view/design-t2-sa/home. Under review at IROS 2026
summary: Assistive robots operating under shared autonomy must balance user control with autonomous assistance. Because robot actions depend on internal intent inference that is not directly observable, mismatches between inferred and intended goals can undermine coordination and trust. We investigate how interface-level transparency, including feedback modality (visual vs. auditory) and information richness (sparse vs. rich), shapes interaction in a vision-based shared autonomy system. In a user study with N=25 participants across two assistive manipulation tasks, we evaluate how these designs influence coordination and trust. Providing feedback significantly improves intent alignment and reduces corrective intervention, indicating that making the inferred goal legible accelerates convergence in shared control. Participants preferred visual over auditory feedback, while preferences for sparse versus rich information depended on task complexity. We also found that revealing the full belief distribution did not consistently improve alignment or trust. Together, these findings indicate that effective transparency enhances coordination primarily through goal legibility, while trust depends on task-appropriate information exposure rather than maximal disclosure. Based on these results, we outline guidelines for designing transparent shared autonomy systems.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how interface transparency (visual vs. auditory feedback, sparse vs. rich information) affects coordination and trust in shared autonomy assistive robots, finding that feedback improves goal alignment but trust depends on task-appropriate disclosure rather than maximum information.
AI.motivation: Shared autonomy robots rely on internal intent inference that is not directly observable, leading to mismatches between inferred and intended goals that undermine coordination and trust.
AI.method: User study with N=25 participants across two assistive manipulation tasks, comparing visual vs. auditory feedback and sparse vs. rich information transparency designs.
AI.result: Feedback significantly improves intent alignment and reduces corrective intervention; participants prefer visual over auditory feedback; sparse vs. rich preferences depend on task complexity; revealing full belief distribution does not consistently improve alignment or trust.
AI.conclusion: Effective transparency enhances coordination through goal legibility, while trust depends on task-appropriate information exposure rather than maximal disclosure. Guidelines for designing transparent shared autonomy systems are provided.

title: USU-Corn-WeedDB: A UAV RGB Image Dataset for Multi-Species Weed Detection in Forage Corn
summary: Weed pressure in forage corn production causes yield losses of up to 31.5%, yet site-specific weed management (SSWM) systems built on UAV imagery and deep learning remain constrained by the scarcity of field-representative training datasets. We present USU-Corn-WeedDB, a publicly available UAV RGB image dataset collected from a commercial forage corn field in Cache Valley, Utah, designed to support multi-class weed detection under both supervised and semi-supervised learning frameworks. RGB imagery was acquired on 27 June 2025 using an Autel EVO II Dual 640T V2 drone at ~10m above ground level, yielding a ground sampling distance of approximately 0.48 cm/pixel. A total of 366 full-resolution images were tiled into 8,800 patches at 640 x 640-pixel resolution. Of these, 800 images were manually annotated for three weed species; common lambsquarters (Chenopodium album), redroot pigweed (Amaranthus retroflexus), and green foxtail (Setaria viridis) comprising 10,539 bounding-box instances, with the remaining 8,000 tiles retained as an unlabeled pool for semi-supervised experiments. This dataset reflects a natural class imbalance where redroot pigweed constitutes 53.86% of annotated instances, which was preserved intentionally to mirror real field conditions. To validate dataset utility, we trained 28 object detection models spanning five architecture families including YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv10, YOLO11, YOLO26, and RT-DETR under identical conditions without hyperparameter tuning. Test set mAP@0.5 ranged from 0.773 to 0.840, with lightweight models achieving competitive performance relevant to edge-deployed UAV systems. USU-Corn-WeedDB is publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20044178.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces USU-Corn-WeedDB, a publicly available UAV RGB image dataset for multi-class weed detection in forage corn, containing 800 annotated and 8,000 unlabeled image patches. It covers three weed species with intentional class imbalance (redroot pigweed dominant at 53.86%). Tests of 28 detection models (YOLOv8-v11, RT-DETR) under identical conditions achieved mAP@0.5 scores of 0.773–0.840, with lightweight models showing promise for edge-deployed UAV systems.
AI.motivation: Weed pressure causes up to 31.5% yield loss in forage corn, but SSWM systems using UAV imagery and deep learning are limited by the scarcity of field-representative training datasets. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing a publicly available, real-world dataset.
AI.method: UAV RGB imagery was collected at ~10m altitude (0.48 cm/pixel GSD) on 27 June 2025. 366 full-resolution images were tiled into 8,800 patches (640×640). 800 patches were manually annotated for three weed species (10,539 bounding boxes), and 8,000 tiles were kept as an unlabeled pool. 28 object detection models from six architecture families (YOLOv8-YOLO26, RT-DETR) were trained without hyperparameter tuning to benchmark the dataset.
AI.result: Redroot pigweed accounts for 53.86% of annotated instances. Test set mAP@0.5 ranged from 0.773 to 0.840 across 28 models. Lightweight models achieved competitive performance suitable for edge-deployed UAV systems.
AI.conclusion: USU-Corn-WeedDB is a valuable public resource for advancing SSWM in forage corn, supporting both supervised and semi-supervised learning. Its class imbalance reflects real field conditions. The dataset is available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20044178.

title: Towards Serverless Semi-Decentralized Federated Learning with Heterogeneous Optimizers
comment: Under review at IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking
summary: We investigate cluster formation, involving the number and composition of clusters, in decentralized federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous machine learning (ML) optimizers. While clustering in centralized FL has enabled scalability and resource savings, its value and development in fully decentralized environments have yet to be explored. Optimizing cluster formation in such environments is challenging, especially due to the complex coupling between network graph structures, local data heterogeneity, and different local ML model optimizers. To address these challenges, we propose serverless semi-decentralized FL (SSD-FL), a methodology requiring no persistent server infrastructure. In SSD-FL, cluster formation occurs via a lightweight, one-time device-to-device (D2D) initialization phase, after which actual ML model training (alongside consensus and convergence processes) is fully serverless. Functionally, SSD-FL segments global rounds into intra-cluster and inter-cluster regimes, ensuring global convergence and consensus through novel "effective loss functions" that integrate device-specific ML optimizers with network graph-based regularization. Next, SSD-FL leverages the consensus gap via the Cheeger inequality to develop an iterative clustering algorithm evaluated against our derived convergence and consensus bounds, which incorporate a unique scoring metric to quantify data and optimizer heterogeneity across devices. Finally, experimental evaluation against three categories of decentralized FL methodologies validate that SSD-FL improves both convergence speeds and communication efficiency across various network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer regimes.

AI.tldr: SSD-FL is a serverless semi-decentralized federated learning framework that forms device clusters via a one-time D2D initialization phase and uses effective loss functions with Cheeger inequality-based clustering to handle heterogeneous ML optimizers, improving convergence and communication efficiency.
AI.motivation: Cluster formation in decentralized FL with heterogeneous ML optimizers is underexplored, and optimizing it is challenging due to the coupling between network graphs, data heterogeneity, and different local optimizers.
AI.method: Proposes SSD-FL: (1) lightweight one-time D2D initialization for cluster formation; (2) intra-cluster and inter-cluster regimes with effective loss functions integrating device-specific optimizers and graph regularization; (3) iterative clustering via Cheeger inequality and consensus gap analysis; (4) scoring metric quantifying data and optimizer heterogeneity.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation against three categories of decentralized FL methods shows SSD-FL improves convergence speeds and communication efficiency across various network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer regimes.
AI.conclusion: SSD-FL effectively addresses the challenges of cluster formation in decentralized FL with heterogeneous optimizers, achieving better convergence and communication efficiency without requiring persistent server infrastructure.

title: Time series Foundation Models based on Physics-Informed Synthetic Histories for Cold-Start Photovoltaic Forecasting
comment: To be published in the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: At commissioning time, Photovoltaic (PV) operators must forecast production before target-site observations are available, limiting the direct use of standard supervised forecasters. This cold-start setting is addressed with a zero-shot pipeline that generates a synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates, enabling time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast through inference-time conditioning. Five TSFMs are benchmarked against classical baselines under strict Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback strategies. The evaluation spans $440$ PV sites across four datasets and diverse climate regimes. Covariate-aware foundation models outperform baselines by approximately $1.7-2\times$: TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE $0.514$, RMSE $0.721$ $kWh$ ${kWp}^{-1}$ ${d}^{-1}$), while Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source, indicating that accuracy is driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific generator.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a zero-shot pipeline that generates synthetic PV production history from plant metadata and weather data, enabling time-series foundation models to forecast in cold-start settings without real observations. Covariate-aware models like TabPFN-TS and Chronos-2 outperform classical baselines by 1.7-2×, with performance driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific synthetic data generator.
AI.motivation: PV operators need to forecast production at commissioning time when no historical observations exist (cold-start setting), which prevents the use of standard supervised forecasters. The authors aim to address this limitation by enabling zero-shot forecasting using synthetic data and foundation models.
AI.method: A zero-shot pipeline generates synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates. Five time-series foundation models (TSFMs) are benchmarked against classical baselines under three strategies: Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback. Evaluation covers 440 PV sites across four datasets and diverse climate regimes.
AI.result: Covariate-aware foundation models outperform classical baselines by approximately 1.7-2×. TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE 0.514, RMSE 0.721 kWh kWp⁻¹ d⁻¹). Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source.
AI.conclusion: The proposed zero-shot pipeline effectively enables PV production forecasting in cold-start settings. Accuracy is driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific synthetic data generator, suggesting broad applicability of this approach.

title: Declarative Skills for AI Agents in Knowledge-Grounded Tool-Use Workflows
summary: We study orchestration mechanisms for tool-using AI agents in realistic customer-service workflows over an unstructured knowledge base. We argue that declarative agents -- AI agents equipped with natural-language skill files appended to the system prompt -- are an effective orchestration paradigm. Concretely, we compare (i) a DeclarativeAgent that reads three domain-specific skill files at inference time and decides its own control flow, (ii) an ImperativeAgent based on a programmatic state machine with explicit phases, and (iii) an unscaffolded baseline agent modeled after the $τ$-Knowledge benchmark agent. Our ImperativeAgent is motivated by externalised-control inference as in Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration frameworks. We formalise the three agents as policy classes within a decentralised partially-observable Markov decision process and analyse their information-theoretic and structural properties; we then test the predicted differences empirically on five language models and two retrieval regimes. Our results show that retrieval quality is a dominant bottleneck for AI agents: when evidence is incomplete or skewed, all agents degrade substantially, and skill files cannot recover lost performance. Under high-quality retrieval, however, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative state machine's brittleness does not reliably improve task success or compliance.

AI.tldr: The paper compares declarative vs. imperative orchestration for tool-using AI agents in customer-service workflows, finding that retrieval quality is the dominant bottleneck, but declarative skill files improve accuracy and reduce errors under high-quality retrieval.
AI.motivation: To understand effective orchestration mechanisms for tool-using AI agents in realistic customer-service workflows over unstructured knowledge bases.
AI.method: The authors compare three agent types (DeclarativeAgent, ImperativeAgent, unscaffolded baseline) formalized as policy classes within a decentralized partially-observable Markov decision process, tested on five language models and two retrieval regimes.
AI.result: Retrieval quality is the dominant bottleneck; under high-quality retrieval, declarative skills improve accuracy and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative state machine does not reliably improve task success.
AI.conclusion: Declarative agents with natural-language skill files are an effective orchestration paradigm, but retrieval quality remains critical for overall performance.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Learning for Long-Horizon Task Planning Under Complex Logical Constraints
summary: Task planning often suffers from severe efficiency bottlenecks when robots must reason over long-horizon action sequences under complex logical constraints, including object affordances, spatial relationships, and sequential action dependencies. Recent neuro-symbolic methods improve planning efficiency by learning object-importance scores to prune task-irrelevant objects, but they typically rely on fixed offline supervision generated from full search spaces. This creates a train-test mismatch: at deployment, the planner operates in pruned search spaces induced by the model's own imperfect predictions, leading to exposure bias and degraded planning performance. To address this challenge, we formulate object-importance learning for task planning as an imperative learning-based bilevel optimization problem. The upper level optimizes a neural scorer, while the lower level solves a symbolic planning problem in the score-pruned search space. To stabilize this learning process, we introduce a 3R strategy into the lower-level planning, using parallel Repair, Restart, and Rollback recovery to provide reliable and adaptive feedback for upper-level learning. Experiments on three challenging benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, including an 80.04% reduction in failure rate and a 57.14% reduction in planning time. We further validate the framework on a quadruped-based mobile manipulator in simulation and the real world, demonstrating its potential for efficient and deployable neuro-symbolic task planning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an imperative learning-based bilevel optimization framework for object-importance learning in task planning, using a 3R strategy (Repair, Restart, Rollback) to stabilize training and address exposure bias, achieving 80% failure rate reduction and 57% planning time reduction on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic planning methods suffer from train-test mismatch (exposure bias) because they use fixed offline supervision from full search spaces, but deployment uses pruned spaces from imperfect model predictions, degrading performance.
AI.method: Formulates object-importance learning as a bilevel optimization problem (upper: neural scorer; lower: symbolic planning in pruned space), with a novel 3R strategy (Repair, Restart, Rollback) in the lower level to provide stable and adaptive feedback.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on three benchmarks: 80.04% failure rate reduction, 57.14% planning time reduction. Validated on a quadruped-based mobile manipulator in both simulation and real world.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively resolves exposure bias in neuro-symbolic task planning, demonstrating efficient and deployable performance in both benchmark and real-world robotic settings.

title: Anchored, Not Graded: Vision-Language Models Fail at Slant-from-Texture Perception
summary: Human perception of surface slant from texture exhibits systematic, graded biases that emerge reliably in psychophysical experiments. Prior work showed that unsupervised CNNs reproduce several human-like biases, while supervised CNNs do not. Do Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit similar competences? Across multiple VLM families and model scales, zero-shot and in-context prompting both produce distinctive failures: slant is predicted at only a small set of anchors (e.g., 0\degree, $\pm$25\degree, $\pm$45\degree) with little dependence on stimulus field of view, optical slant, or surface curvature. Supervised fine-tuning partially remediates the failure, but residual anchoring persists. While success in high-level vision-language benchmarks might not require sensitivity to low-level geometric cues, we interpret anchoring as a failure at the representation-to-output language interface: Not necessarily an absence of geometric encoding, but a failure to express it in a graded form.

AI.tldr: VLMs fail to reproduce human-like graded perception of surface slant from texture, instead producing "anchored" predictions at discrete angles like 0°, ±25°, ±45°, with little sensitivity to visual parameters. Supervised fine-tuning helps only partially.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed unsupervised CNNs reproduce human-like biases in slant perception from texture, but supervised CNNs do not. This paper investigates whether Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit similar human-like competences.
AI.method: Tested multiple VLM families and scales using zero-shot and in-context prompting, plus supervised fine-tuning, on psychophysical slant-from-texture tasks.
AI.result: VLMs produce anchored slant predictions (e.g., 0°, ±25°, ±45°) with little dependence on field of view, optical slant, or curvature. Fine-tuning only partially resolves this anchoring.
AI.conclusion: VLMs' failure is not necessarily a lack of geometric encoding, but a problem at the representation-to-language interface—they cannot express graded geometric information properly.

title: The Geography of Algorithmic Judgment: LLM Intermediaries, Place Identity, and Racial Steering in Housing Search
comment: 13 pages with supplemental tables and figures, AIES '26 Submission
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly assuming an intermediary role in housing search through the integration of listing platforms within conversational interfaces, mediating access to information, search, and recommendations within urban settings. We expand on prior work on racial steering in LLMs by conducting a behavioral audit of seven open-weight and closed-source LLMs across four U.S. cities, testing location recommendations across three iterative prompting conditions that progressively add lifestyle preference context and reflect fair housing paired-testing methodologies. We find that steering is an emergent behavior of the model's interpretive license rather than primarily a static property. Steering results from the interaction of a user's identity, preference articulation, and the spatial logic that a model has internalized about learned representations of place, preference, and opportunity in a given city, and how different types of users relate to it. While steering was present, it was not uniform in direction or magnitude across evaluated conditions. Preference-conditioned testing often increased or reconfigured the number of models that exhibited steering behaviors relative to baseline conditions, suggesting that LLMs may interpret what the same housing preference means differently depending on the racial identity of the user. Our findings also demonstrate that the city is not a neutral testing unit for LLM evaluation in place-based sectors, and results from one local market cannot be assumed to generalize to another. Local and domain expertise will be required in the housing sector to ensure that legal and institutional commitments to fair housing are not undermined while adopting AI tools that mediate spatial access.

AI.tldr: This paper audits seven LLMs across four U.S. cities for racial steering in housing recommendations, finding that steering is an emergent behavior from the interaction of user identity, preference articulation, and the model's internalized spatial logic, with results varying by city and prompting condition.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs exhibit racial steering in housing recommendations, expanding on prior work by examining how identity, preference context, and city-specific spatial logic interact to produce steering behaviors.
AI.method: Behavioral audit of seven open-weight and closed-source LLMs across four U.S. cities, using iterative prompting with three conditions (baseline, lifestyle preference context) and reflecting fair housing paired-testing methodologies.
AI.result: Steering is emergent and not uniform; preference-conditioned testing increased or reconfigured steering behaviors; results do not generalize across cities; LLMs interpret same preferences differently based on user racial identity.
AI.conclusion: Local and domain expertise is required to ensure fair housing commitments are not undermined when adopting AI tools that mediate spatial access.

title: Quantum-Inspired Trace-Augmented Evidence Selection for Reasoning over Structured Hypothesis Spaces
summary: Large language models (LLMs) now solve a wide range of expert-level exams at or above human level, yet remain brittle on specialised, evidence-intensive domains such as law. On these tasks, errors arise not only from gaps in world knowledge but also from subtle distinctions between pieces of evidence and inconsistent use of supporting evidence. The most common aggregator over sampled chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, majority vote, returns the most popular answer regardless of whether its evidence is actually strongest. We propose to treat the selection of CoT reasoning fragments into a set of evidence as an explicit combinatorial optimisation problem, allowing well-supported but minority hypotheses to override noisy majorities, and to evaluate the approach on legal-reasoning benchmarks that are particularly sensitive to evidence quality. We introduce EP-HUBO (Evidence Pool Higher-Order Binary Optimisation), which generates multiple CoT traces with a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, solves a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation per pool with quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness), and delegates a single adjudication call per question to a frontier model. We evaluate EP-HUBO on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks using both simulated annealing on classical hardware and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine from Quantum Computing Inc. HUBO-style optimisation gives a principled way to aggregate reasoning fragments while preserving minority-but-correct hypotheses, and is most valuable in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not already absorbed the benchmark material.

AI.tldr: EP-HUBO treats evidence selection from CoT traces as an optimization problem, using a small local model to generate evidence pools and a frontier model for final adjudication, outperforming majority vote on legal reasoning benchmarks—especially when benchmark contamination is low.
AI.motivation: LLMs are brittle on evidence-intensive domains like law because majority voting over CoT traces picks the most popular answer regardless of evidence quality, and minority-but-correct hypotheses get discarded.
AI.method: EP-HUBO: (1) generate multiple CoT traces with a small local model, (2) parse fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, (3) solve a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) with weights for relevance, specificity, distinctiveness, (4) delegate a single adjudication call per question to a frontier model. Optimization is done via simulated annealing or the Dirac-3 photonic quantum machine.
AI.result: EP-HUBO outperforms majority vote on evidence-intensive legal benchmarks, especially in low-contamination settings where frontier models have not memorized the benchmark data.
AI.conclusion: HUBO-style optimization provides a principled aggregation of reasoning fragments that preserves minority-but-correct hypotheses and is most valuable for specialized, low-contamination domains.

title: A Cross-view Fusion Framework for Robust 6-DoF Grasp Pose Estimation
summary: In this paper, we propose a cross-view fusion framework that enhances the robustness of 6-DoF grasp pose estimation in corner views. Our framework alleviates occlusion by incorporating an auxiliary view and avoids the time-consuming, task-agnostic multi-view reconstruction through a post-fusion strategy. To enhance cross-view fusion, we propose a self-supervised contrastive learning strategy that leverages cross-view associations to regularize point cloud features. In brief, a cross-view point pair is considered a match if the two points correspond to the same 3D location, and a non-match if they represent distinct grasp directions. The learning strategy significantly enhances the spatial consistency and direction distinctiveness of point features, thereby facilitating cross-view fusion and improving estimation robustness. Furthermore, we propose a cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module to fuse grasp-relevant geometry into a comprehensive representation. Specifically, the module first aligns the cross-view points and features according to their similarity to enhance the robustness against noise. Subsequently, these points are registered into the cylindrical coordinate frame, emphasizing the rotation-symmetric geometry which is important for grasping. Finally, local self-attention and seed cross-attention layers are alternately employed, respectively enabling interactions within single views and across views, which supports fine-grained representation of grasp-relevant geometry. Our framework achieves strong performance on the GraspNet-1Billion benchmark and in real-world applications. Code is available at https://github.com/KJZhuAutomatic/Cross-view-Grasp.

AI.tldr: A cross-view fusion framework for robust 6-DoF grasp pose estimation in corner views, using self-supervised contrastive learning and a cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module, achieving strong performance on GraspNet-1Billion and real-world applications.
AI.motivation: Existing grasp pose estimation methods suffer from occlusion in corner views, and multi-view reconstruction is time-consuming and task-agnostic. The authors aim to enhance robustness by incorporating an auxiliary view without costly reconstruction.
AI.method: (1) Self-supervised contrastive learning leveraging cross-view associations (matching same 3D location vs. distinct grasp directions) to regularize point cloud features. (2) Cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module: aligns points/features by similarity, registers into cylindrical coordinates (emphasizing rotation-symmetric geometry), and uses local self-attention + seed cross-attention for cross-view interaction.
AI.result: Achieves strong performance on the GraspNet-1Billion benchmark and in real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cross-view fusion framework effectively improves 6-DoF grasp estimation robustness under occlusion, validated both on benchmark and real-world scenarios.

title: MedSIGHT: Towards Grounded Visual Comprehension in Medical Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical large vision-language models (Med-LVLMs) have recently achieved remarkable progress in vision-language comprehension and medical image segmentation. However, existing models still struggle to unify these two capabilities, which is essential for achieving clinically reasoning that connects visual findings with semantic interpretation. We present MedSIGHT, a unified framework that equips Med-LVLMs with structured, pixel-level understanding for grounded visual comprehension. MedSIGHT introduces a novel Region Perceiver module that produces region-centric tokens, encoding spatial information directly into representation space of the language model. We further propose a medical region codebook into the LLM vocabulary, allowing the model to generate discrete region codes as symbolic representations of anatomical and pathological regions. These codes are decoded through the Region Perceiver to reconstruct segmentation mask, achieving end-to-end spatial grounding. Lastly, MedSIGHT combines Region Perceiver, Codebook and LLM using our proposed progressive training strategy to gradually aligns these modules stably. Trained on only 72K multimodal instruction pairs, MedSIGHT achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse imaging modalities on both medical comprehension and segmentation tasks.

AI.tldr: MedSIGHT is a unified framework that combines medical vision-language understanding with pixel-level segmentation by introducing a Region Perceiver module and a medical region codebook, enabling structured spatial grounding. It achieves SOTA on both comprehension and segmentation tasks across diverse medical imaging modalities.
AI.motivation: Existing Med-LVLMs cannot unify vision-language comprehension and medical image segmentation, which is essential for clinically reasoning that connects visual findings with semantic interpretation.
AI.method: MedSIGHT introduces (1) a Region Perceiver module for region-centric tokens encoding spatial info into LLM representation space, (2) a medical region codebook in LLM vocabulary for discrete region codes as symbolic representations, and (3) a progressive training strategy to stably align modules.
AI.result: Trained on only 72K multimodal instruction pairs, MedSIGHT achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse imaging modalities on both medical comprehension and segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework effectively bridges visual comprehension and segmentation in Med-LVLMs, enabling grounded visual understanding critical for clinical reasoning.

title: RECAP: Regression Evaluation for Continual Adaptation of Prompts
summary: Production agentic systems routinely face evolving constraints and must comply from the very next interaction. Scenarios like a tool-call notification changing a compliance threshold or a policy update adding disclosure requirements fit this criteria, having close to no room for errors in production. This proactive adaptation setting is common in deployment, but absent from current benchmarks, which assume either static constraint sets or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback. We introduce RECAP, a benchmark that measures continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level under a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize before seeing any test data. Evaluating six methods across four LLMs and three schedules with evolving constraints, we find that these methods show no significant improvement in performance, even after incurring a higher latency. These methods, designed for offline or reactive settings, are inadequate for the proactive paradigm. Our work emphasizes the growing need for designing proactive prompt adaptation methods, where the models must remain robust to evolving needs in deployment.

AI.tldr: RECAP benchmark evaluates proactive prompt adaptation under evolving constraints, finding current methods fail to improve performance despite higher latency, highlighting need for new approaches designed for proactive settings.
AI.motivation: Production agentic systems face evolving constraints (e.g., policy updates, compliance changes) with no room for errors, yet current benchmarks assume static or reactive protocols, lacking evaluation of proactive adaptation where models must adapt before seeing test data.
AI.method: RECAP benchmark employs a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive constraint specifications and must generalize before encountering test data. Six methods are evaluated across four LLMs and three schedules with evolving constraints, measuring continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level.
AI.result: Current prompt optimization methods show no significant improvement in performance under proactive adaptation, even with higher latency costs. Methods designed for offline or reactive settings are inadequate for the proactive paradigm.
AI.conclusion: There is a growing need for designing proactive prompt adaptation methods where models remain robust to evolving deployment needs, as existing approaches fail in this critical production scenario.

title: Accounting for Context: Shaping Moral Credences for Value Alignment
summary: Ensuring that agent behaviours are aligned with human moral values inevitably raises the problem of how to account for the plurality of moral perspectives that societies -- and even individuals -- typically adopt. Work on moral uncertainty proposes mechanisms to fairly and democratically aggregate evaluations of actions across different moral theories.
