AI.result: Consistent improvements over standard contrastive learning on ImageNet, ExDark, and PASCAL VOC for classification and detection, with the same architecture and training budget. Also shows promise in supervised learning and simpler lighting settings.
AI.conclusion: Lighting-aware training enhances model robustness and adaptability in complex visual environments and conventional image processing tasks, suggesting broad applicability beyond complex illumination scenarios.

title: The Geometry of Last-Layer Model Stealing
summary: This paper uses geometry to explain how a machine learning model can be stolen using an already existing well-known method. The author has shown the exact conditions required to perfectly copy the final layer of a transformer network. When looking deeper into the hidden layers the author has explained clear limits. The author has also demonstrated that a hidden network cannot be fully reverse engineered just by looking at the final results. The research clearly maps out what can and cannot be stolen from a model.

AI.tldr: This paper uses geometry to analyze the limits of stealing machine learning models, showing that while the final layer of a transformer can be perfectly copied under certain conditions, hidden layers cannot be fully reverse engineered from final outputs.
AI.motivation: To understand the exact conditions and limits of model stealing attacks, mapping out what can and cannot be stolen from a machine learning model.
AI.method: Geometric analysis to study the feasibility of copying transformer layers, examining both final and hidden layers.
AI.result: The final layer can be perfectly copied under specific conditions, but hidden layers cannot be fully reverse engineered from final outputs.
AI.conclusion: There are clear boundaries to model stealing: final layers are vulnerable, but hidden layers remain protected from complete reverse engineering.

title: How AI Agents Reshape Knowledge Work: Autonomy, Efficiency, and Scope
summary: Frontier AI systems are bridging the gap between intelligence and utility by shifting from conversational assistants to autonomous agents that execute tasks end to end. Using production data from Perplexity's Search and Computer products, we study this transition by examining how AI agents accelerate and reshape knowledge work. Three key empirical findings emerge. First, using sessions with near-identical initial query pairs as natural experiments for the same underlying task attempted with both products, Computer performs 26 minutes of autonomous work per user session, versus 33 seconds for Search. Computer automates task decomposition and execution that Search users might otherwise manually orchestrate and implement. As a result, Computer shifts follow-up query distribution toward higher-order work such as verification and extension. Autonomy also increases execution quality, with per-query dissatisfaction rates 55% lower on Computer than on Search. Second, due to its autonomy advantage, Computer reduces completion time from 269 to 36 minutes on matched tasks, lowering estimated time and cost by 87% and 94%, respectively, compared to humans equipped with Search alone. Third, Computer changes the scope of work that users attempt: Computer queries more often cross occupational boundaries, require higher-order cognition, draw on broader expertise, take the form of composite tasks that bundle interdependent subtasks into a single query, and unlock work activities that are essentially absent from Search usage among the same users. Together, the evidence indicates that AI agents accelerate workflows, enhance output quality, reduce costs, and expand the breadth and depth of automated work.

AI.tldr: AI agents (Computer) outperform conversational AI (Search) by autonomously executing 26 minutes of work per session vs. 33 seconds, reducing time by 87% and cost by 94%, lowering dissatisfaction by 55%, and expanding the scope of automated knowledge work into higher-order, cross-boundary tasks.
AI.motivation: To study how AI agents transitioning from conversational assistants to autonomous agents reshape knowledge work, using production data from Perplexity's Search and Computer products.
AI.method: Analysis of production data from Perplexity's Search and Computer products; used near-identical initial query pairs as natural experiments to compare the same underlying tasks across both products.
AI.result: (1) Computer performs 26 min of autonomous work per session vs. 33 sec for Search, with 55% lower dissatisfaction; (2) reduces completion time from 269 to 36 min, lowering time by 87% and cost by 94%; (3) shifts work toward higher-order, cross-occupational, composite tasks absent from Search usage.
AI.conclusion: AI agents accelerate workflows, enhance output quality, reduce costs, and expand the breadth and depth of automated knowledge work.

title: An Abstract Architecture for Explainable Autonomy in Hazardous Environments
comment: Originally published 20th of October 2022 at the Second International Workshop on Requirements Engineering for Explainable Systems (RE4ES), which was hosted by the International Requirements Engineering Conference 2022
summary: Autonomous robotic systems are being proposed for use in hazardous environments, often to reduce the risks to human workers. In the immediate future, it is likely that human workers will continue to use and direct these autonomous robots, much like other computerised tools but with more sophisticated decision-making. Therefore, one important area on which to focus engineering effort is ensuring that these users trust the system. Recent literature suggests that explainability is closely related to how trustworthy a system is. Like safety and security properties, explainability should be designed into a system, instead of being added afterwards. This paper presents an abstract architecture that supports an autonomous system explaining its behaviour (explainable autonomy), providing a design template for implementing explainable autonomous systems. We present a worked example of how our architecture could be applied in the civil nuclear industry, where both workers and regulators need to trust the system's decision-making capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an abstract architecture for designing explainable autonomous systems that can explain their behavior, aimed at building trust with human users in hazardous environments like the nuclear industry.
AI.motivation: To ensure human workers trust autonomous robotic systems used in hazardous environments, as explainability is closely linked to trustworthiness and should be designed into systems from the start rather than added afterward.
AI.method: The authors propose an abstract architecture that supports explainable autonomy, serving as a design template for implementing systems that can explain their own behavior.
AI.result: A worked example demonstrating how the proposed architecture could be applied in the civil nuclear industry context, addressing trust needs of both workers and regulators.
AI.conclusion: Explainability should be designed into autonomous systems from the outset, and the proposed architecture provides a practical template for achieving trustworthy, explainable autonomy in high-stakes environments.

title: LUCID: Learning Unified Control for Image Deflaring and Exposure Mastery in Nighttime Photography
summary: Photography is the art of painting with light, yet nighttime scenes are shaped by competing degradations: intense flares obscure scene structure, while photon-limited regions collapse into noise. Conventional approaches address these factors in isolation, overlooking the fact that these degradations are fundamentally entangled. To bridge this gap, we introduce LUCID, a unified framework that reframes nighttime restoration as a continuous and controllable process rather than a fixed correction. We decompose nighttime restoration into two cooperative components: a flare disentanglement module that lifts the 'curtain' of optical artifacts to provide reliable structural guidance, and a diffusion-driven module that leverages generative priors to reconstruct clean and well-exposed imagery. Crucially, LUCID introduces explicit controllability through a novel four-mode training strategy, enabling users to steer the restoration process via classifier-free guidance (CFG) and allowing selective control over light sources and their associated flare and ghosting artifacts, while also supporting high dynamic range (HDR) reconstruction through continuous exposure control. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LUCID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse real-world nighttime scenarios.

AI.tldr: LUCID is a unified framework for nighttime image restoration that jointly handles flares and noise through a flare disentanglement module and diffusion-driven reconstruction, with controllable four-mode training enabling selective artifact control and HDR exposure adjustment.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat nighttime degradations (flares and photon-limited noise) in isolation, overlooking their fundamental entanglement.
AI.method: A two-component framework: (1) a flare disentanglement module that removes optical artifacts to provide structural guidance, and (2) a diffusion-driven module using generative priors for clean reconstruction. A four-mode training strategy enables classifier-free guidance (CFG) for selective control over light sources, flares, ghosting, and continuous HDR exposure control.
AI.result: LUCID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse real-world nighttime scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Nighttime restoration is better framed as a continuous, controllable process rather than a fixed correction, with cooperative handling of entangled degradations.

title: Modeling Nonlinear Feature Interactions with Product-Unit Residual Networks
summary: Understanding nonlinear feature interactions is crucial in science and engineering, yet standard multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) often capture such interactions only implicitly, leading to entangled representations that can impair robustness and interpretability. We investigate product-unit residual networks (PURe) that integrate multiplicative product units with residual connections to explicitly model cross-feature couplings while stabilizing optimization. We conduct a systematic evaluation on an interaction-driven synthetic benchmark and two real-world datasets, assessing predictive accuracy, robustness to Gaussian feature noise, and performance under limited training data, and we compare real- and complex-valued variants under a matched parameter budget. Beyond accuracy, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP)-based interaction analyses show that PURe learns more concentrated and structurally coherent interaction patterns than MLP baselines. Overall, PURe achieves competitive or improved performance, better robustness and sample efficiency in low-data regimes, and enhanced interaction-level interpretability.

AI.tldr: PURe networks integrate multiplicative product units with residual connections to explicitly model nonlinear feature interactions, achieving competitive accuracy, better robustness to noise, improved sample efficiency, and more interpretable interaction patterns compared to standard MLPs.
AI.motivation: Standard MLPs capture nonlinear feature interactions only implicitly, leading to entangled representations that impair robustness and interpretability. The authors aim to explicitly model cross-feature couplings while stabilizing optimization.
AI.method: Product-Unit Residual Networks (PURe) integrate multiplicative product units with residual connections. Evaluated on synthetic benchmarks and real-world datasets, compared against real- and complex-valued variants under matched parameter budgets, using SHAP for interaction analysis.
AI.result: PURe achieves competitive or improved predictive accuracy, better robustness to Gaussian feature noise, and superior sample efficiency under limited training data. SHAP analysis shows PURe learns more concentrated and structurally coherent interaction patterns than MLP baselines.
AI.conclusion: PURe offers a promising alternative to standard MLPs for applications where explicit modeling of nonlinear feature interactions, robustness, sample efficiency, and interpretability are important.

title: Robotic Policy Adaptation via Weight-Space Meta-Learning
summary:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are emerging as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation, enabling general-purpose policies trained from large corpora of demonstrations and action labels. However, adapting these models to new tasks still typically requires task-specific demonstrations, action annotations, and additional fine-tuning, making deployment costly and difficult to scale.
  We propose WIZARD, a weight-space meta-learning framework that sidesteps task-specific fine-tuning by generating task-specific LoRA parameters for a frozen VLA policy. Given only a language instruction and a short demonstration video, WIZARD predicts the corresponding adaptation weights in a single forward pass, without target-task action labels or test-time optimization. During meta-training, WIZARD learns to map task evidence directly to expert LoRA updates, capturing relationships between tasks in weight space.
  Experiments on LIBERO show that WIZARD improves performance by up to ~2x on unseen dataset collections and up to ~14x on unseen tasks. On a Franka Emika Panda, WIZARD consistently improves over a real-domain adapted baseline, showing that generated adapters provide task-level specialization beyond simulation.

AI.tldr: WIZARD is a weight-space meta-learning framework that generates task-specific LoRA parameters for frozen VLA policies from just a language instruction and demonstration video, eliminating the need for task-specific action labels or fine-tuning, achieving up to 14x improvement on unseen tasks.
AI.motivation: VLA models require task-specific demonstrations, action annotations, and fine-tuning for new tasks, making deployment costly and difficult to scale.
AI.method: WIZARD uses weight-space meta-learning to predict task-specific LoRA parameters for a frozen VLA policy in a single forward pass, given only a language instruction and short demonstration video, without test-time optimization or action labels.
AI.result: Up to ~2x improvement on unseen dataset collections and up to ~14x improvement on unseen tasks in LIBERO; consistent improvement on real Franka Emika Panda robot.
AI.conclusion: Generated adapters provide task-level specialization beyond simulation, enabling efficient adaptation without costly fine-tuning.

title: Beyond Skeletons: Learning Animation Directly from Driving Videos with Same2X Training Strategy
summary: Human image animation aims to generate a video from a static reference image, guided by pose information extracted from a driving video. Existing approaches often rely on pose estimators to extract intermediate representations, but such signals are prone to errors under occlusion or complex poses. Building on these observations, we present DirectAnimator, a framework that bypasses pose extraction and directly learns from raw driving videos. We introduce a Driving Cue Triplet consisting of pose, face, and location cues that captures motion, expression, and alignment in a semantically rich yet stable form, and we fuse them through a CueFusion DiT block for reliable control during denoising. To make learning dependable when the driving and reference identities differ, we devise a Same2X training strategy that aligns cross-ID features with those learned from same-ID data, regularizing optimization and accelerating convergence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DirectAnimator attains state-of-the-art visual quality and identity preservation while remaining robust to occlusions and complex articulation, and it does so with fewer computational resources. Our project page is at https://directanimator.github.io/.

AI.tldr: DirectAnimator is a human image animation framework that bypasses pose extraction by learning directly from raw driving videos using a Driving Cue Triplet (pose, face, location cues) fused via a CueFusion DiT block, with a Same2X training strategy to handle cross-ID scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer computational resources.
AI.motivation: Existing human image animation methods rely on pose estimators for intermediate representations, which are error-prone under occlusion or complex poses. The authors aim to bypass pose extraction and learn directly from raw driving videos to improve robustness.
AI.method: DirectAnimator introduces (1) a Driving Cue Triplet (pose, face, location cues) for rich yet stable motion expression, (2) a CueFusion DiT block for reliable denoising control, and (3) a Same2X training strategy that aligns cross-ID features with same-ID data for better generalization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art visual quality and identity preservation, robustness to occlusions and complex articulation, and lower computational resource requirements compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Bypassing pose extraction and directly learning from raw driving videos is a viable and superior approach for human image animation, achieving better robustness and efficiency.

title: Product units in gated recurrent units improve nuclear-mass prediction
summary: The prediction of masses of atomic nuclei using machine learning can complement theoretical models and advance the exploration of poorly known domains of the nuclear chart. We propose a machine learning technique based on gated recurrent units (GRU), which have demonstrated competitive performance in nuclear-mass prediction by exploiting long-term dependencies. By integrating multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations within recurrent units, we report significant improvements in nuclear-mass prediction. Computations are performed in the complex domain to jointly capture amplitude and phase dynamics. For interpolation and temporal-extrapolation tasks based on the atomic mass evaluation (AME2016 and AME2020), the complex additive-multiplicative product-unit gated recurrent unit (AM-PU-GRU) model consistently achieves the lowest prediction errors, with an interpolation RMSE of 0.227 $\pm$ 0.004 MeV and an extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 $\pm$ 0.015 MeV. These results surpass other state-of-the-art machine learning models and also outperform the real-valued GRU baseline and product-unit ablation variants, while remaining robust to different theoretical priors, including WS4 and SEMF. Our findings establish complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks as a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction.

AI.tldr: A complex-valued gated recurrent unit (GRU) with additive-multiplicative product units (AM-PU-GRU) achieves state-of-the-art nuclear mass prediction, outperforming other ML models with interpolation RMSE of 0.227 MeV and extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 MeV on AME2016/AME2020 data.
AI.motivation: Machine learning can complement theoretical models to predict atomic nuclear masses, especially in poorly known regions of the nuclear chart, where traditional methods may be limited.
AI.method: Proposes a complex-valued AM-PU-GRU that integrates multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations within recurrent units, operating in the complex domain to jointly capture amplitude and phase dynamics.
AI.result: Achieves interpolation RMSE of 0.227 ± 0.004 MeV and extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 ± 0.015 MeV, outperforming state-of-the-art ML models, real-valued GRU baselines, and product-unit ablation variants, while remaining robust to different theoretical priors (WS4, SEMF).
AI.conclusion: Complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks establish a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction.

title: Beyond Waypoints: A Trajectory-Centric Waypointing Paradigm for Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments (VLN-CE) requires agents to follow natural-language instructions while navigating in real-world-like environments. Most VLN-CE approach\-es adopt a three-stage framework: a waypoint predictor proposes navigable waypoints, and a navigator selects the best waypoint, with a low-level controller executing the movement to it. However, this decoupled paradigm often leads to unreachable waypoints or inconsistencies between planning and control. In this work, instead of predicting isolated waypoints, we introduce a novel paradigm called Trajectory Waypoint, which grounds each candidate waypoint in an executable trajectory. To realize this, we design a Trajectory Waypoint Predictor formulated as a TSDF-guided diffusion policy, which steers trajectory generation away from obstacles, inherently ensuring the reachability of the predicted waypoints. We further propose a trajectory-enhanced navigator that injects the associated trajectory as additional information for planning, enabling strict consistency between high-level semantic decisions and low-level execution. Extensive experiments on the VLN-CE benchmark show that our Trajectory Waypoint paradigm achieves superior performance over the baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Trajectory Waypoint, a new paradigm for VLN-CE that replaces isolated waypoints with executable trajectories using a TSDF-guided diffusion policy, improving reachability and consistency between planning and control.
AI.motivation: The decoupled three-stage framework (waypoint predictor → navigator → controller) used in most VLN-CE approaches leads to unreachable waypoints and inconsistencies between high-level planning and low-level control.
AI.method: Proposes a Trajectory Waypoint Paradigm: (1) a Trajectory Waypoint Predictor using TSDF-guided diffusion policy to generate executable trajectories avoiding obstacles, (2) a trajectory-enhanced navigator that uses trajectory information for consistent planning.
AI.result: Superior performance over baselines on the VLN-CE benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Grounding waypoints in executable trajectories and using trajectory information for navigation planning improves both reachability and consistency in VLN-CE tasks.

title: polyDAG: Polynomial Acyclicity Constraints for Efficient Continuous Causal Discovery in Visual Semantic Graphs
summary: Modern image-analysis pipelines often convert images into structured semantic variables, such as facial attributes, object concepts, and scene descriptors. Learning directed dependencies among these variables can produce interpretable visual semantic graphs, but continuous directed acyclic graph learning is limited by the cost of enforcing acyclicity. We present polyDAG, a polynomial acyclicity framework for efficient continuous causal discovery in visual semantic graphs. polyDAG replaces the matrix-exponential acyclicity constraint with a finite polynomial trace constraint and proves that the new constraint is zero exactly for acyclic graphs. We further derive a geometric-series implementation that avoids the explicit summation loop while preserving the same acyclicity condition. Experiments on synthetic Erdos-Renyi graphs and CelebA facial visual attributes show that polyDAG improves efficiency and structure recovery. Averaged over the revised synthetic protocol with d in {100, 200, 500}, polyDAG reduces mean structural Hamming distance from 318.4 to 285.4 and improves mean F1 score from 0.725 to 0.756. At 100 nodes, the geometric variant runs in 3.44 seconds compared with 5.16 seconds for the exponential baseline, corresponding to a 33.4 percent speedup. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/wenhaoz-fengcai/polyDAG.

AI.tldr: polyDAG introduces a polynomial acyclicity constraint (replacing matrix-exponential) for efficient continuous causal discovery in visual semantic graphs, achieving faster runtime (33.4% speedup) and better structure recovery (F1 improvement from 0.725 to 0.756) on synthetic and CelebA datasets.
AI.motivation: Continuous directed acyclic graph (DAG) learning for visual semantic graphs is limited by the high computational cost of enforcing acyclicity using matrix-exponential constraints.
AI.method: Replaces the matrix-exponential acyclicity constraint with a finite polynomial trace constraint, proving it is zero exactly for acyclic graphs. Also derives a geometric-series implementation that avoids explicit summation loops.
AI.result: On synthetic graphs (d=100/200/500), mean SHD reduces from 318.4 to 285.4, F1 improves from 0.725 to 0.756. At 100 nodes, geometric variant runs 3.44s vs 5.16s (33.4% speedup).
AI.conclusion: polyDAG offers a more efficient and accurate alternative for continuous causal discovery in visual semantic graphs, with publicly available code.

title: Evidence-Grounded Ensemble Diagnosis of 802.11 Packet Captures: A Multi-Stage Pipeline with Deterministic Reliability Scoring
summary:
Diagnosing 802.11 packet captures requires expert protocol knowledge, is slow, inconsistent across engineers, and unscalable. LLM-based approaches sound plausible but fabricate protocol events absent from captures (especially truncated traces), produce uncalibrated confidence scores, and suffer evaluation bias when golden references are co-produced by the model under test. We introduce PROBE (Protocol Reasoning Over evidence-Based Ensembles), a multi-stage pipeline addressing all three failures. It integrates (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability, (ii) multi-run, multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinion and progressive obfuscation, (iii) a verdict-aware evidence framework treating absence of failure evidence as contributing evidence, and (iv) a fully deterministic composite reliability score from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement without LLM self-assessment.
  On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures (104 capture-reviewer pairs), single-pass LLM analysis raises weighted evidence F1 from 0.871 (expert baseline) to 0.912 but misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops below baseline (0.842) as majority voting amplifies conservative verdicts: 50% of confirmed failures are misclassified as 'no issue' or 'insufficient evidence.' Adding evidence-grounded reconciliation achieves 0.957 F1, a 96% auto-accept rate, and a worst-case floor above 0.70. LLM self-reported confidence clusters at 0.95 regardless of difficulty (71% report exactly 0.95), confirming it is uninformative. We also introduce a model-agnostic evaluation framework using per-field assertion matching, eliminating circular bias from model-co-produced golden references.

AI.tldr: PROBE is a multi-stage pipeline for diagnosing 802.11 packet captures that addresses LLM failures like hallucination, uncalibrated confidence, and evaluation bias. It achieves 0.957 weighted evidence F1 (vs 0.871 expert baseline) and 96% auto-accept rate using deterministic normalization, multi-run ensembles, verdict-aware evidence, and a composite reliability score without LLM self-assessment.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing 802.11 packet captures is slow, inconsistent, and requires expert protocol knowledge. Existing LLM approaches fabricate events, produce uncalibrated confidence scores, and suffer evaluation bias from model-co-produced golden references.
AI.method: PROBE integrates: (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability, (ii) multi-run multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinion and progressive obfuscation, (iii) verdict-aware evidence framework treating absence of failure evidence as contributing evidence, and (iv) a composite reliability score from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement.
AI.result: On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures, PROBE achieves 0.957 weighted evidence F1 (vs 0.871 expert baseline), 96% auto-accept rate, and worst-case floor above 0.70. Single-pass LLM misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops to 0.842 F1. LLM self-reported confidence is uninformative (71% report exactly 0.95).
AI.conclusion: PROBE effectively addresses LLM failures in protocol diagnosis through evidence-grounded reconciliation, achieving superior performance over both expert baselines and naive LLM approaches while introducing a model-agnostic evaluation framework to eliminate circular bias.

title: CAPE: Contrastive Action-conditioned Parallel Encoding for Embodied Planning
summary: Embodied agents need to predict the future consequences of candidate actions in order to plan effectively before execution. Existing visual dynamics models learn by reconstructing future visual states or rolling out dense latent representations, which spreads learning capacity across visually salient but planning-irrelevant content rather than the action-conditioned changes that drive manipulation outcomes. We propose CAPE, a Contrastive Action-conditioned Parallel Encoding framework that learns visual dynamics by distinguishing the future outcomes induced by different action sequences. Given an initial observation and a candidate action sequence, CAPE decodes the full future latent trajectory in a single forward pass and is trained with a Goal-Convergent Contrastive Objective that aligns predictions corresponding to the same future outcome while separating those corresponding to different outcomes. On real-world DROID and zero-shot transfer to RoboCasa, CAPE substantially outperforms prior baselines on future-state retrieval, offline action matching, and closed-loop planning, while notably reducing planning-time inference cost at long prediction horizons.

AI.tldr: CAPE is a contrastive learning framework for visual dynamics that predicts future action outcomes efficiently by focusing on action-conditioned changes rather than reconstructing all visual details.
AI.motivation: Existing visual dynamics models waste capacity on reconstructing visually salient but planning-irrelevant content, rather than focusing on action-conditioned changes critical for manipulation outcomes.
AI.method: CAPE decodes full future latent trajectories in a single forward pass using a Goal-Convergent Contrastive Objective that aligns same-outcome predictions and separates different-outcome predictions.
AI.result: On DROID and RoboCasa, CAPE outperforms baselines on future-state retrieval, offline action matching, and closed-loop planning, with reduced inference cost for long horizons.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive action-conditioned parallel encoding is an effective and efficient approach for learning visual dynamics for embodied agent planning.

title: DRIFT: From Robustness Gaps to Invariance Manifolds for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of generative image models challenges existing AI-generated image detectors, particularly in open-world settings with unseen generators. Recent training-free approaches measure robustness gaps in frozen vision foundation models (VFMs), detecting fakes via perturbation-induced embedding drift. However, these methods rely on fixed invariance geometry inherited from pretraining and lack principled adaptation to the detection task. We instead formulate AI-generated image detection as learning a structured invariance manifold of real images under one-class supervision. Building upon a frozen VFM, we introduce lightweight projection heads that decompose representation space into complementary robust and fragile subspaces. The robust subspace is explicitly trained to suppress variations induced by physically plausible imaging transformations, approximating tangent directions of a real-image manifold, while the fragile subspace retains sensitivity to edit-like perturbations. A structured ordering margin enforces hierarchical separation between physical invariance and edit-induced variability, enabling detection as a margin-violation test relative to the learned manifold. At inference, multi-scale patch-wise drift under both transformation families yields a dual-channel invariance signature and interpretable localization. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong open-world generalization across unseen generators and resolutions, consistently outperforming training-free robustness-based baselines while providing interpretable invariance-violation maps.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a training-based method for AI-generated image detection that learns a structured invariance manifold of real images using frozen vision foundation models, outperforming training-free approaches in open-world settings.
AI.motivation: Existing training-free detection methods rely on fixed invariance geometry inherited from pretraining and lack principled adaptation to the detection task, limiting their effectiveness in open-world settings with unseen generators.
AI.method: Introduces lightweight projection heads on frozen VFMs to decompose representation into robust (physically invariant) and fragile (edit-sensitive) subspaces. A structured ordering margin enforces hierarchical separation, and multi-scale patch-wise drift yields a dual-channel invariance signature for detection.
AI.result: Strong open-world generalization across unseen generators and resolutions, consistently outperforming training-free robustness-based baselines while providing interpretable invariance-violation maps.
AI.conclusion: Learning a structured invariance manifold with one-class supervision is more effective for AI-generated image detection than relying on fixed pretrained invariance geometry.

title: GlucoFM-Bench: Benchmarking Time-Series Foundation Models for Blood Glucose Forecasting
summary: Blood glucose forecasting models are foundational for modern diabetes management systems, as reliable short-term predictions can enable proactive interventions, support automated insulin delivery, and reduce the risk of hypo- and hyperglycemic events. From a modeling perspective, glucose forecasting poses unique challenges due to heterogeneous physiological dynamics across diabetes populations. Traditional machine learning and deep learning models have been extensively evaluated for glucose prediction, yet recent time-series foundation models (TSFMs) remain much less studied in this setting. To bridge this gap, we present GlucoFM-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark evaluating state-of-the-art TSFMs alongside supervised deep learning models for blood glucose forecasting. We assess eight representative architectures, including pre-trained TSFMs, time-series large language models, and task-specific deep learning models, across 15 publicly available diabetes-relevant datasets comprising 1,117 individuals with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, prediabetes, and no diabetes. Models are evaluated under zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols, with systematic variation in context length and prediction horizon. Across datasets, pre-trained TSFMs, especially Chronos-2 and TimesFM, show strong zero-shot and few-shot transfer, with the best zero-shot model performing within 5% of the best full-shot supervised model. Yet, when task-specific data are abundant, a lightweight LSTM remains strongest, outperforming TSFMs by 4--21% under full-shot training. Stratified analyses reveal persistent challenges in T1D cohorts and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges, highlighting the need for evaluation beyond aggregate error metrics. Together, GlucoFM-Bench provides a standardized and reproducible foundation for evaluating, comparing, and improving foundation models for blood glucose forecasting.

AI.tldr: GlucoFM-Bench benchmarks 8 time-series models (including foundation models like Chronos-2, TimesFM vs. LSTM) on 15 diabetes datasets with 1,117 individuals. Pre-trained TSFMs excel in zero/few-shot settings (within 5% of best supervised), but a lightweight LSTM outperforms them by 4-21% when full training data is available. Challenges persist in T1D and extreme glucose ranges.
AI.motivation: Blood glucose forecasting is critical for diabetes management, but time-series foundation models (TSFMs) remain understudied in this domain despite their success in other areas. This paper aims to bridge that gap by systematically evaluating TSFMs against traditional deep learning models.
AI.method: The authors construct GlucoFM-Bench, evaluating 8 models (pre-trained TSFMs, time-series LLMs, supervised deep learning models) across 15 public datasets (1,117 individuals). Models are tested under zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols with varying context lengths and prediction horizons.
AI.result: Pre-trained TSFMs (Chronos-2, TimesFM) achieve strong zero/few-shot performance (within 5% of best supervised model). However, with abundant task-specific data, a lightweight LSTM outperforms TSFMs by 4-21%. Stratified analysis shows persistent challenges in T1D cohorts and extreme glucose ranges.
AI.conclusion: GlucoFM-Bench provides a standardized benchmark for glucose forecasting. While TSFMs offer strong transfer capabilities, task-specific models (LSTM) still dominate when data is plentiful. Evaluation beyond aggregate metrics is needed, especially for T1D and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges.

title: RhinoVLA Technical Report
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for robotic manipulation, but real-time deployment on edge hardware remains challenging. In this work, we identify VLM visual and context tokens as a major source of deployment latency: for GEMM-dominated projection operators, computation grows linearly with the number of input tokens when model dimensions are fixed. Motivated by this observation, we propose RhinoVLA, a deployment-oriented VLA model co-designed with the Huixi R1 edge SoC. RhinoVLA adopts a token-efficient Qwen3-VL backbone and a continuous Action Expert, reducing the VLM-side token and computation burden while preserving pretrained multimodal capability. To support cross-robot learning, RhinoVLA further introduces a unified interface that combines View Registry, 72D physical state-action slot space, and robotinstance LoRA, allowing heterogeneous robot observations and action schemas to be aligned under a shared policy. On the deployment side, RhinoVLA is optimized through hardware-aware compilation, mixed-precision execution, and parallel visual encoding. Experiments show that RhinoVLA achieves downstream performance comparable to π0.5 at a similar parameter scale, while reaching 11.69 Hz end-to-end inference on Huixi R1, meeting the 10 Hz real-time closedloop control target. The project will be open-sourced at https://github.com/HuixiAI/RhinoVLA.

AI.tldr: RhinoVLA is a deployment-oriented VLA model co-designed with the Huixi R1 edge SoC that reduces token/computation burden via a token-efficient Qwen3-VL backbone and continuous Action Expert, supports cross-robot learning with a unified interface, and achieves 11.69 Hz real-time inference on edge hardware while matching π0.5 performance.
AI.motivation: VLA models show strong potential for robotic manipulation but suffer from high deployment latency on edge hardware due to VLM visual and context tokens causing linear computation growth in GEMM-dominated projection operators.
AI.method: RhinoVLA adopts a token-efficient Qwen3-VL backbone and continuous Action Expert to reduce VLM-side token/computation burden; introduces a unified interface (View Registry, 72D physical state-action slot space, robot-instance LoRA) for cross-robot learning; and optimizes deployment via hardware-aware compilation, mixed-precision execution, and parallel visual encoding on Huixi R1 edge SoC.
AI.result: RhinoVLA achieves 11.69 Hz end-to-end inference on Huixi R1 (meeting 10 Hz real-time target) with performance comparable to π0.5 at similar parameter scale.
AI.conclusion: RhinoVLA demonstrates that co-designing VLA models with edge hardware can achieve real-time closed-loop control without sacrificing downstream task performance, enabling practical edge deployment for robotic manipulation.

title: SVHighlights: Towards Extremely Long Sport Video Highlight Detection
comment: Accepted to KDD 2026 (Datasets and Benchmarks Track). Project Page: https://leedongkyu2019.github.io/SVHighlights/
summary: While highlight detection for long-form videos is of great practical importance, most existing methods remain limited to short-form content, largely due to the absence of a suitable benchmark. To bridge this gap, we introduce SVHighlights, to the best of our knowledge, the first benchmark for highlight detection in extremely long sports videos, each exceeding one hour in duration, across multiple sports categories. SVHighlights is constructed from pairs of full-length sports videos and their corresponding official highlight videos using a dataset generation pipeline, enabling scalable label generation without conventional per-clip saliency annotation. The benchmark comprises 320 videos with an average duration of 2.00 hours and a total of 640.18 hours, substantially exceeding previous datasets. Existing methods also face fundamental challenges on long videos: models trained on short clips fail to generalize to hour-long content, and their clip-level scoring lacks the broader context needed to identify highlights. To address this and provide a strong baseline, we present TF-SELECTOR, a training-free segment-based approach that divides each video into context-aware segments by merging adjacent shots sharing the same semantic content, and predicts segment-level saliency scores using a large language model with multimodal inputs including visual captions, transcripts, and audio volume. Experiments demonstrate that TF-SELECTOR achieves superior performance across most metrics compared to Video Temporal Grounding (VTG)-tuned baselines, with improvements of +3.12 in HIT@1, +4.06 in HIT@K, and +2.95 in IoU. These results establish SVHighlights as a challenging testbed for long-form highlight detection and demonstrate that a simple segment-based strategy can effectively scale to hour-long videos.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SVHighlights, the first benchmark for highlight detection in extremely long sports videos (320 videos, avg 2 hrs each, total 640 hrs), and proposes TF-SELECTOR, a training-free segment-based method using LLMs with multimodal inputs that outperforms VTG-tuned baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing highlight detection methods are limited to short-form content due to the lack of a suitable benchmark for long-form videos. There is a practical need for detecting highlights in hour-long sports videos.
AI.method: SVHighlights is constructed from full-length sports videos paired with official highlight videos using a dataset generation pipeline for scalable labeling. TF-SELECTOR divides videos into context-aware segments by merging adjacent shots sharing the same semantic content, then predicts segment-level saliency using a large language model with multimodal inputs (visual captions, transcripts, audio volume).
AI.result: TF-SELECTOR achieves +3.12 in HIT@1, +4.06 in HIT@K, and +2.95 in IoU improvements over VTG-tuned baselines on the SVHighlights benchmark.
AI.conclusion: SVHighlights serves as a challenging testbed for long-form highlight detection, and a simple segment-based strategy can effectively scale to hour-long videos without training.

title: Data-Constrained Language Model Pretraining: Improved Regularization and Scaling Laws
summary: Classical scaling laws for language model pretraining balance model size against training dataset size under a fixed compute budget, assuming abundant data and a single pass over the corpus. As training compute grows faster than the supply of natural language data, pretraining is likely to enter a data-constrained, compute-rich regime where models train for multiple epochs over a finite dataset. We study data-constrained pretraining along two axes, regularization and scaling. For regularization, we study masked-input regularization (MIR), an auxiliary next-token prediction loss on randomly masked inputs. MIR tests whether the random masking central to diffusion language models can benefit autoregressive pretraining without architectural changes or inference overhead. Across 72M to 1.4B parameter models, we find that MIR added on top of strong weight decay improves validation loss over autoregressive strong-weight-decay-only models, with downstream gains at 1.4B. For scaling, we propose SoftQ, a scaling law that couples model size and data size to capture their interaction under repeated data. Classical alternatives such as the Chinchilla law use an additive form that decouples these terms, making them misspecified in the data-constrained regime. We find that SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than these alternatives, and estimates MIR's gains as equivalent to roughly 1.3 times as much unique training data. We release our code at https://github.com/yixinw-lab/dc_pretrain.

AI.tldr: This paper studies data-constrained pretraining with two contributions: (1) Masked-input regularization (MIR) improves autoregressive language models under data scarcity, and (2) SoftQ, a new scaling law that better captures model-data interaction under repeated epochs, showing MIR is equivalent to ~1.3x more unique data.
AI.motivation: As training compute grows faster than natural language data supply, pretraining will enter a data-constrained, compute-rich regime where models train for multiple epochs over finite datasets. Classical scaling laws (e.g., Chinchilla) assume abundant data and a single pass, making them misspecified for this scenario.
AI.method: (1) Masked-input regularization (MIR): an auxiliary next-token prediction loss on randomly masked inputs, tested on 72M to 1.4B parameter models, combined with strong weight decay. (2) SoftQ: a scaling law that couples model size and data size multiplicatively to capture their interaction under repeated data, as opposed to additive forms like Chinchilla.
AI.result: MIR improves validation loss over strong weight decay alone, with downstream gains at 1.4B. SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than classical alternatives, and estimates MIR's gains as equivalent to roughly 1.3 times more unique training data.
AI.conclusion: Data-constrained pretraining requires new regularization and scaling approaches. MIR provides a simple, inference-free improvement, and SoftQ offers a better scaling law for this regime. Code is released.

title: Spline Policy: A Structured Representation for Robot Policies
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Modern imitation-learning policies for robot manipulation often represent actions as fixed-resolution action chunks, which are simple and effective but expose limited geometric and temporal structure before execution. This paper studies Spline Policy (SP), a structured representation that replaces action chunks with spline parameters while keeping the policy backbone unchanged. The predicted spline can be decoded as a compact continuous trajectory, queried at different temporal resolutions, constrained or edited in parameter space, and passed to downstream controllers. For quadratic spline outputs, the same representation can also be converted into a state-dependent vector field through an analytical distance-field construction. Under the regularity and projection assumptions of this construction, the induced dynamics do not increase the distance to the generated spline, yielding a principled local corrective mechanism around the predicted motion. The spline output further supports uncertainty propagation from observations to spline parameters, trajectories, and flow fields, and can be combined with classical control mechanisms such as null-space collision avoidance without retraining the policy backbone. We instantiate SP with diffusion, flow-matching, transformer-based, and vision-language-action backbones. Experiments in low-dimensional motion learning, simulated manipulation under matched backbones, dexterous manipulation, and real-robot case studies show that SP remains compatible with modern policy learners while exposing useful motion-structure properties, including compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction around predicted motions, uncertainty evaluation, and controller compatibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Spline Policy (SP), a representation that replaces discrete action chunks with continuous spline parameters for robot manipulation policies, enabling compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction, uncertainty propagation, and controller compatibility without changing the policy backbone.
AI.motivation: Modern imitation-learning policies use fixed-resolution action chunks that lack geometric and temporal structure. The authors aim to introduce a more structured representation (spline-based) that exposes these properties while keeping existing policy backbones unchanged.
AI.method: SP replaces action chunks with spline parameters as the policy output. The spline decodes into a continuous trajectory, supports temporal resampling, parameter-space editing, and can be converted into a state-dependent vector field via analytical distance-field construction. This construction provides a local corrective mechanism. SP is instantiated with diffusion, flow-matching, transformer-based, and vision-language-action backbones.
AI.result: Experiments in low-dimensional motion learning, simulated manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and real-robot case studies show SP remains compatible with modern policy learners while exposing motion-structure properties like compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction, uncertainty evaluation, and controller compatibility.
AI.conclusion: Spline Policy is a versatile, structured alternative to action chunks that enhances robot manipulation policies with continuous motion structure and useful control properties without requiring backbone changes.

title: When CLIP Sees More, It Fights Back Harder: Multi-View Guided Adaptive Counterattacks for Test-Time Adversarial Robustness
summary: Vision-language models such as CLIP have achieved remarkable zero-shot recognition capabilities, yet their robustness against adversarial perturbations remains limited. Test-time counterattack (TTC) was recently proposed to improve CLIP's robustness by perturbing an input image to steer it away from a corrupted state during inference. However, TTC remains fragile under strong attacks because its counterattack relies on a directly corrupted original view and employs a noise-driven hard-gating scheme that cannot adapt to varying corruption severity. To address these limitations, we introduce Multi-view guided Adaptive Counterattack (MAC), which performs counterattacks for multi-view with corruption-aware soft weighting. Specifically, MAC first constructs augmented views of an input image to obtain diverse embeddings. It then performs counterattacks to refine corrupted embeddings of views. Next, MAC adaptively scales the counterattack intensity for each view based on its estimated corruption degree. Finally, the adaptively counterattacked views are aggregated to yield a robust final prediction. Extensive experiments across 20 datasets and diverse attack scenarios demonstrate that MAC substantially improves robustness while preserving high inference speed and memory efficiency with its tuning-free design. Our code is available at https://github.com/sunoh-kim/MAC.

AI.tldr: MAC improves CLIP's adversarial robustness via multi-view counterattacks with corruption-aware soft weighting, without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing TTC method for CLIP is fragile under strong attacks due to its reliance on a single corrupted view and a noise-driven hard-gating scheme that cannot adapt to varying corruption severity.
AI.method: MAC constructs augmented multi-view embeddings, performs counterattacks per view, adaptively scales attack intensity based on estimated corruption degree, and aggregates views for robust prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 20 datasets show substantial robustness improvements with high inference speed and memory efficiency, thanks to tuning-free design.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view guided adaptive counterattack is an effective, tuning-free approach to significantly enhance CLIP's robustness against adversarial attacks.

title: GRASP: Geometry-aware Residual Alignment for Scalable Pretraining Data Attribution
summary: Scalable data attribution methods typically assign isolated utility scores to individual training examples. This prevalent additive assumption fundamentally fails to capture critical subset dynamics, including data redundancy and complementary coverage. In this work, we reframe attribution as subset-level counterfactual utility prediction and introduce GRASP, an interaction-aware surrogate. Grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound, GRASP explicitly models subset interactions through a quadratic geometric penalty. To achieve pretraining-scale efficiency without relying on hidden oracle tuning, we couple low-dimensional feature sketches with a strictly finite lower-confidence bound selection protocol. Extensive subset-retraining evaluations demonstrate that GRASP decisively outperforms existing scalable baselines. It more than doubles the task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity while reducing upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Downstream diagnostics further show that this scoring mechanism transfers to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection, establishing a robust foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora.

AI.tldr: GRASP introduces an interaction-aware attribution method that models subset-level dynamics (e.g., redundancy, complementarity) using a quadratic geometric penalty, achieving superior counterfactual fidelity and efficiency at pretraining scale.
AI.motivation: Existing scalable data attribution methods rely on additive assumptions (assigning isolated scores to individual examples), which fundamentally fail to capture critical subset dynamics like data redundancy and complementary coverage.
AI.method: GRASP reframes attribution as subset-level counterfactual utility prediction. It uses a quadratic geometric penalty grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound to model subset interactions, coupled with low-dimensional feature sketches and a strictly finite lower-confidence bound selection protocol for efficiency without hidden oracle tuning.
AI.result: GRASP more than doubles task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity while reducing upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Transfers effectively to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection.
AI.conclusion: GRASP provides a robust, scalable foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora by explicitly modeling subset interactions, outperforming existing baselines decisively.

title: Simulation-Driven Imitation Learning for Biosignals-Free Shared-Autonomy Prosthetic Grasping
summary: Biosignals-free shared-autonomy control of upper-limb prosthetic hands aims to enable natural and low-effort manipulation without relying on EMG or other physiological signals. Recent imitation-learning-based approaches have shown promising results, but their scalability is limited by the cost and variability of collecting large amounts of real-world human demonstration data. In this work, we present a scalable simulation framework that automatically generates diverse reach-to-grasp demonstrations from a wrist-mounted virtual camera. The framework combines physically feasible grasp synthesis, natural reaching trajectories retargeting, and reach--grasp--lift execution in procedurally generated indoor environments. It records wrist-view observations, proprioception, and actions to build a large-scale demonstration dataset for imitation learning. Through extensive simulation benchmarks, we evaluate object and scene generalization and compare several representative state-of-the-art imitation learning methods. Results show that the simulated demonstrations are sufficiently rich and consistent for effective policy learning. In three realistic settings, the learned sim-to-real policy achieves over 90\% grasp success, surpasses baseline methods, and exhibits stronger generalization, highlighting the promise of simulation-driven training for biosignals-free shared-autonomy prosthetic grasping. The demonstrations are available at \href{https://sites.google.com/view/sim-prosthetic-grasp/home}{https://sites.google.com/view/sim-prosthetic-grasp/home}.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a scalable simulation framework that automatically generates diverse reach-to-grasp demonstrations for training imitation-learning-based shared-autonomy control of upper-limb prosthetic hands, achieving over 90% grasp success in sim-to-real transfer without relying on biosignals.
AI.motivation: Existing imitation-learning-based approaches for biosignals-free prosthetic hand control show promise but suffer from scalability issues due to the high cost and variability of collecting large-scale real-world human demonstration data.
AI.method: The framework combines physically feasible grasp synthesis, natural reaching trajectory retargeting, and reach-grasp-lift execution in procedurally generated indoor environments, using a wrist-mounted virtual camera to record observations, proprioception, and actions for imitation learning.
AI.result: In simulation benchmarks and three realistic settings, the learned policy achieves over 90% grasp success, surpasses baseline methods, and demonstrates stronger object and scene generalization.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-driven training is a promising approach for biosignals-free shared-autonomy prosthetic grasping, offering scalable and effective policy learning without costly real-world data collection.

title: SS-TPT: Stability and Suitability-Guided Test-Time Prompt Tuning for Adversarially Robust Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP achieve strong zero-shot recognition but remain highly fragile under adversarial perturbations. Recent test-time adaptation defenses improve robustness by leveraging many augmented views, but this leads to impractical slowdown and a clear robustness-throughput trade-off. To address this challenge, we present Stability and Suitability-guided Test-time Prompt Tuning (SS-TPT), evaluating the quality of each augmented view via two complementary scores: (1) stability, measuring prediction invariance to weak augmentations, and (2) suitability, measuring feature-space density among views. These stability and suitability (SS) scores guide both adaptation and inference through an SS-guided consistency loss and an SS-weighted prediction, amplifying trustworthy views while suppressing corrupted ones. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SS-TPT significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness-throughput trade-offs across diverse datasets and varying numbers of views, thereby demonstrating both strong practicality and generality. Our code is available at https://github.com/sunoh-kim/SS-TPT.

AI.tldr: SS-TPT improves VLM robustness against adversarial perturbations by using stability and suitability scores to guide test-time prompt tuning, achieving better robustness-throughput trade-offs than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs like CLIP are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, and current test-time adaptation defenses that use many augmented views suffer from impractical slowdown and a poor robustness-throughput trade-off.
AI.method: SS-TPT evaluates each augmented view via two scores: (1) stability (prediction invariance to weak augmentations) and (2) suitability (feature-space density among views). These guide adaptation via an SS-guided consistency loss and inference via SS-weighted prediction, amplifying trustworthy views and suppressing corrupted ones.
AI.result: SS-TPT significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness-throughput trade-offs across diverse datasets and varying numbers of views.
AI.conclusion: SS-TPT demonstrates strong practicality and generality for test-time adversarial defense in VLMs, offering an efficient solution without sacrificing robustness.

title: TALAN: Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks for Targeted Post-Training of Large Language Models
summary:
Targeted post-training aims to improve reasoning, math, and code without degrading strengths. Low-rank adapters are efficient but task-global; activation interventions are input-aware but often require separate probes, vectors, or inference-time steering. We introduce TALAN (Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks), a sequence-conditioned latent side path inserted into a transformer's residual stream and co-trained with a low-rank adapter in one SFT loop. TALAN compresses the active sequence into latent memory, remixes it into token-level perturbations, and writes them back through a controlled residual update. It is configured along six axes: insertion location, memory size, mixer, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale.
  Across four Qwen3-family backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks, TALAN improves matched LoRA and DoRA baselines. With LoRA, it yields a +1.41 point cross-model mean gain, positive on all four backbones and non-negative on all 16 model-benchmark cells. With DoRA, it yields a +1.85 point mean gain, positive on all backbones and on 13 of 16 cells. Paired seed checks support positive average effects but show nontrivial variance, so we treat them as sensitivity checks. Cost is small: <1% trainable parameters relative to the backbone and 1.01-1.02x inference overhead versus matched LoRA. A Llama-3.2-1B transfer probe is also positive under LoRA and rsLoRA across seven paired seeds, supporting a transfer beyond Qwen.
  Internal-state analyses suggest TALAN is a small complementary activation intervention. The matched adapter update is 80-1,700x larger than the TALAN perturbation, yet their directions have near-zero cosine; per-layer measurements show this small orthogonal perturbation propagates and amplifies through depth. TALAN offers a practical platform for studying steerable activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training.

AI.tldr: TALAN inserts a latent side path into transformer residual streams, compressing sequence information into task-aligned perturbations that complement low-rank adapters, improving STEM/code reasoning by ~1.4-1.9 points across Qwen3 backbones with <1% parameter overhead and ~1.01-1.02x inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods face a trade-off: low-rank adapters (LoRA/DoRA) are efficient but task-global and cannot adapt to individual inputs, while activation interventions are input-aware but require separate probes, vectors, or inference-time steering, complicating the training pipeline.
AI.method: TALAN inserts a sequence-conditioned latent side path into a transformer's residual stream, compressing the active sequence into latent memory, remixing it into token-level perturbations, and writing them back via controlled residual updates. It is co-trained with a low-rank adapter in one SFT loop, configurable along six axes: insertion location, memory size, mixer, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale.
AI.result: Across Qwen3 backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks: +1.41 mean gain over LoRA (positive on all 4 backbones, non-negative on all 16 model-benchmark cells); +1.85 mean gain over DoRA (positive on all 4 backbones, 13/16 cells). Inference overhead is only 1.01-1.02x versus matched LoRA with <1% extra parameters. Positive transfer also seen on Llama-3.2-1B.
AI.conclusion: TALAN provides a practical, lightweight platform for input-aware activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training. The adapter update is 80-1,700x larger than TALAN's perturbation, yet their directions have near-zero cosine, suggesting the small orthogonal perturbation propagates and amplifies through depth to yield meaningful improvements.

title: Rapid co-design of Buoyancy-assisted robots for Challenging Locomotion using Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists
summary: Designing high-performance legged robots requires jointly optimizing morphology and control. Model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers an alternative to model-predictive control for developing robust controllers without explicitly specifying robot dynamics. Thus, we have seen theuse of RL to train controllers and evaluate designs for robot morphology optimization. While RL has shown success inlocomotion, using it in the co-design inner loop is expensive due to repeated policy training. Universal policies conditioned on morphology offer a promising alternative, but suffer from behavioral diversity collapse, converging to a single strategy that performs sub-optimally across designs. On the other hand, end-to-end Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures fail due to a collapse in its representation. We propose Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists (GES), a framework that decouples design-space partitioning from policy learning to capture diverse behaviors explicitly. GES assigns specialist policies to evolving Gaussian regions and iteratively refines them via training, probing, and territory expansion. The resulting specialists are integrated into a design sampling loop, replacing costly re-training with direct evaluation. When tested on the Buoyancy-Assisted Light Legged Unit (BALLU), GES discovers designs with 5 - 25% higher performance than naive universal policies. On hardware, a GES optimized design overcomes a 24 cm tall obstacle - 3x improvement over the baseline BALLU design. Moreover, GES curtails design optimization time by 37%.

AI.tldr: Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists (GES) framework decouples design-space partitioning from policy learning to optimize both morphology and control for legged robots, achieving 5-25% higher performance than universal policies, 3x obstacle height improvement, and 37% reduction in design optimization time.
AI.motivation: Co-designing morphology and control for legged robots via RL is expensive due to repeated policy training. Universal policies suffer from behavioral diversity collapse, and Mixture-of-Experts architectures also fail due to representation collapse.
AI.method: GES decouples design-space partitioning from policy learning by assigning specialist policies to evolving Gaussian regions, iteratively refining them through training, probing, and territory expansion. Specialists are then integrated into a design sampling loop, replacing costly re-training with direct evaluation.
AI.result: On BALLU robot, GES achieves 5-25% higher performance than universal policies; hardware tests show 3x improvement in obstacle height (24 cm vs baseline); design optimization time reduced by 37%.
AI.conclusion: GES effectively addresses behavioral diversity collapse in co-design, enabling efficient morphology optimization with improved performance and reduced computational cost.

authors: Donald Johann Jr. and Fred Prior
title: When is 3D Worth It? A Resource-Performance Frontier for CNNs and Transformers in Lung CT
summary: Three-dimensional models are widely assumed preferable for volumetric medical imaging, yet their practical value depends on whether performance gains justify added computational cost and complexity. Rather than proposing a new architecture, we study how input dimensionality (2D, 2.5D, 3D) affects model behavior across convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) under a fixed training protocol. Using a leakage-free NLST cohort (n = 1,977) with supporting LIDC-IDRI data, we find that the 2.5D CNN offers the most favorable discrimination-stability trade-off in our comparison (ROC-AUC 0.682, 95% CI [0.546, 0.799]) with a stable operating point. In contrast, 3D CNNs show threshold instability, and transformers exhibit degenerate predictions, such as all-positive predictions. Confidence intervals are wide and overlapping, so we present these results as a controlled resource-performance frontier and a failure-mode taxonomy rather than as definitive superiority claims. For class-imbalanced lung cancer screening classification, 2D and 2.5D inputs provide a more reliable trade-off between performance, stability, and computational efficiency than full 3D representations.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how input dimensionality (2D, 2.5D, 3D) affects CNN and ViT models for lung cancer screening, finding that 2.5D CNNs offer the best stability-efficiency trade-off, while 3D CNNs suffer threshold instability and transformers produce degenerate predictions.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the assumed superiority of 3D models for volumetric medical imaging is practically justified, given their added computational cost and complexity, by systematically comparing performance across 2D, 2.5D, and 3D inputs.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of input dimensionality (2D, 2.5D, 3D) across CNNs and Vision Transformers under a fixed training protocol, using a leakage-free NLST cohort (n=1,977) and LIDC-IDRI data.
AI.result: 2.5D CNN achieved the best discrimination-stability trade-off (ROC-AUC 0.682, 95% CI [0.546, 0.799]), while 3D CNNs showed threshold instability and transformers produced degenerate all-positive predictions; confidence intervals were wide and overlapping.
AI.conclusion: 2D and 2.5D inputs provide a more reliable trade-off between performance, stability, and computational efficiency than full 3D representations for class-imbalanced lung cancer screening classification. Results are presented as a controlled resource-performance frontier and failure-mode taxonomy, not as definitive superiority claims.

title: The Fine-Tuning Trap: Evaluating Negative Transfer and the Role of PEFT in Sub-1B Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Deploying Small Language Models (SLMs) on edge devices requires efficient fine-tuning strategies that adapt models to new tasks without degrading their general capabilities. In this study, we benchmark five sub-1B models (135M-1B) on mathematical reasoning tasks and uncover a critical vulnerability: Full Fine-Tuning (Full FT) actively harms performance in models under 300M parameters, often dropping accuracy below zero-shot baselines. This "negative transfer" makes Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) not just an efficiency preference, but a stability requirement. We find that while Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Weight-Decomposed LoRA (DoRA) perform comparably, their strengths vary by task; DoRA excels in complex reasoning (GSM8K), while LoRA dominates pattern matching (OrcaMath). In particular, Full FT is outperformed by LoRA on aligned models (Qwen2.5-0.5B) and even by simple 5-shot In-Context Learning on the smallest architectures (SmolLM2-135M). Based on these findings, we recommend defaulting to PEFT for all aligned sub-1B models and caution against Full FT for any architecture smaller than 500M parameters to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Reproduction of this work can be found at https://github.com/gulguluu/tiny-slm-finetune-compare.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks sub-1B SLMs on math reasoning and finds Full Fine-Tuning harms models under 300M parameters via negative transfer, making PEFT (LoRA/DoRA) a stability requirement, not just efficiency choice.
AI.motivation: To identify effective fine-tuning strategies for edge-deployed SLMs (<1B parameters) that adapt to new tasks without degrading general capabilities, especially given the vulnerability of small models to catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Benchmark five sub-1B models (135M-1B) on mathematical reasoning tasks (GSM8K, OrcaMath), comparing Full Fine-Tuning vs. PEFT methods (LoRA, DoRA) and 5-shot In-Context Learning.
AI.result: Full FT causes negative transfer in models <300M parameters, dropping below zero-shot baselines. DoRA excels in complex reasoning (GSM8K), LoRA dominates pattern matching (OrcaMath). On aligned 0.5B models, LoRA outperforms Full FT; on 135M models, even 5-shot ICL beats Full FT.
AI.conclusion: Recommend defaulting to PEFT for all aligned sub-1B models and caution against Full FT for any architecture smaller than 500M parameters to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

title: Re-imagining ISO 26262 in the Age of Autonomous Vehicles: Enhancing Controllability through Transferability and Predictability
summary: The ISO 26262 standard defines functional safety for road vehicles through risk assessments based on Severity, Exposure, and Controllability, grounded in a human-driven vehicle paradigm. In the context of autonomous vehicles (AVs), the absence of a human driver necessitates revisiting these principles. This paper decomposes the Controllability placeholder into two auditable evidence dimensions of ISO 26262 by introducing two measurable sub-concepts: Transferability and Predictability. Transferability extends Controllability to capture AV systems' ability to hand off control to dedicated fallback safety mechanisms, while Predictability captures how easily external agents can anticipate AV behavior. Predictability is formally defined from human-robot interaction-inspired principles, and a mathematical framework is provided to quantify it. A designed-versus-achievable gap is introduced to distinguish architectural fallback claims from scene-conditioned achievable fallback capability. The proposed metrics align with ISO 26262 and ISO/PAS 21448 (SOTIF), rendering fallback and interaction claims falsifiable and traceable across ODD slices. These dimensions complement rather than replace existing standards, and the enhancements preserve the structure of ISO 26262 while extending its applicability to driverless automated systems operating at SAE Levels 4 and 5.

AI.tldr: This paper extends ISO 26262 for autonomous vehicles by decomposing the Controllability placeholder into two measurable dimensions: Transferability (hand-off to fallback systems) and Predictability (how well external agents anticipate AV behavior), with formal quantification metrics to make safety claims falsifiable and traceable across ODD slices.
AI.motivation: ISO 26262's Controllability concept assumes a human driver, which breaks down for SAE Level 4/5 autonomous vehicles lacking a human fallback. A measurable, auditable replacement is needed to assess functional safety in driverless systems.
AI.method: The authors decompose Controllability into Transferability (system's ability to hand off to fallback safety mechanisms) and Predictability (how easily external agents can anticipate AV behavior), formalize Predictability via human-robot interaction principles with mathematical quantification, and introduce a designed-versus-achievable gap to distinguish architectural fallback claims from scene-conditioned achievable fallback capability.
AI.result: The proposed metrics align with ISO 26262 and ISO/PAS 21448 (SOTIF), rendering fallback and interaction claims falsifiable and traceable across ODD slices while preserving the original standard's structure.
AI.conclusion: The enhancements extend ISO 26262's applicability to driverless SAE Level 4 and 5 systems by complementing (rather than replacing) existing standards with auditable, measurable safety dimensions.

title: MVSegNet: A Lightweight Boundary-Aware Network for Fetal Lateral Ventricle Segmentation and Atrial Width Estimation in Prenatal Ultrasound
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Code and trained models will be released upon acceptance. Supplementary material available upon request
summary: Fetal ventriculomegaly is assessed by measuring the atrial width of the lateral ventricle in prenatal ultrasound. Accurate segmentation is essential for this measurement, but acoustic shadowing, speckle noise, and poor contrast make it difficult. We developed MVSegNet, a lightweight encoder-decoder network combining multi-scale feature extraction and boundary-aware refinement. The model was trained and evaluated on 584 expert-annotated transventricular ultrasound frames using a 70/15/15 split. Performance was compared against six segmentation baselines using overlap, boundary, and measurement metrics. MVSegNet achieved a Dice score of 80.79%, IoU of 68.47%, Hausdorff distance of 4.07 mm, and atrial width mean absolute error of 3.40 mm. The model contains 2.31 million parameters and runs at 165.6 frames per second on an NVIDIA T4 GPU. MVSegNet outperformed all evaluated baselines on boundary and measurement metrics while maintaining low computational cost, supporting its use in automated fetal ultrasound analysis.

AI.tldr: MVSegNet is a lightweight encoder-decoder network for fetal ventriculomegaly assessment via lateral ventricle segmentation in ultrasound. It combines multi-scale feature extraction and boundary-aware refinement, achieving 80.79% Dice, 4.07 mm Hausdorff distance, and 3.40 mm atrial width MAE with 2.31M parameters at 165.6 FPS, outperforming six baselines.
AI.motivation: Fetal ventriculomegaly assessment relies on accurate lateral ventricle segmentation in prenatal ultrasound, but acoustic shadowing, speckle noise, and poor contrast make this challenging. Existing methods lack sufficient accuracy and efficiency for clinical use.
AI.method: MVSegNet is a lightweight encoder-decoder network that combines multi-scale feature extraction with boundary-aware refinement. It was trained and evaluated on 584 expert-annotated transventricular ultrasound frames using a 70/15/15 split.
AI.result: MVSegNet achieved Dice=80.79%, IoU=68.47%, Hausdorff distance=4.07 mm, atrial width MAE=3.40 mm, with 2.31M parameters and 165.6 FPS on an NVIDIA T4 GPU, outperforming six baselines on boundary and measurement metrics.
AI.conclusion: MVSegNet outperformed all evaluated baselines on boundary and measurement metrics while maintaining low computational cost, supporting its use in automated fetal ultrasound analysis.

title: From Sampled Outcomes to Capability Distributions: Rethinking Supervision for LLM Routing
summary: Existing LLM routing methods typically treat a model's single response to a query as its capability label for training routers. However, because LLM generation is inherently stochastic, such single-shot supervision provides only a noisy observation of a query-model pair's behavior rather than a reliable capability estimate. We show that this assumption introduces systematic noise into routing supervision, making learned routing policies less reliable. To address this issue, we propose DARS (Distribution-Aware Routing Supervision), a framework that constructs routing supervision from a distributional view of model behavior. Instead of relying on a single generated response, DARS considers uncertainty from both the input side and the output side, capturing how semantically equivalent query formulations and stochastic generations affect model performance. Based on these distribution-aware observations, DARS builds more reliable supervision signals for routing. Experiments across diverse tasks show that single-shot labels can be misleading for model selection, while distribution-aware supervision provides more stable labels and improves learned routing behavior. Our results suggest that reliable LLM routing should move beyond single-response observations and be grounded in query-level model capability distributions.

AI.tldr: Existing LLM routing uses single-response labels which are noisy due to LLM stochasticity.
